summary: Read-only 2.0 readiness audit of whisper-transcribe across 10 dimensions, with adversarial bug verification
logs: Analyzed 10 dimensions; 38 candidate bugs; 29 survived adversarial verification

review.currentState:
whisper-transcribe is a single-screen, browser-only Azure Speech transcription tool with no build step. The UI is one HTML page (index.html) with a collapsible Notion-style sidebar (input device, noise toggle, model picker, theme + API settings buttons in the footer), a main column containing a transcript textarea, an audio visualizer canvas, a status line, a timer, and a control cluster (pause, mic, retry, cancel). State is driven by an event bus + RecordingStateMachine; status messaging flows through UI.setStatus / showTemporaryStatus.

First-run flow (settings.js checkInitialSettings, lines 692-712): on load, if no apiKey/uri is stored, after a 500ms delay it shows "Please configure Azure OpenAI settings" and auto-opens the settings modal. The modal (index.html 199-246) lets the user pick a model and enter Target URI + API key with helper text and a Save button. Validation (settings.js getValidationErrors, 543-575) enforces HTTPS, key character set, and a Whisper-specific 32-hex-char format. Recording flow is mic-click to start, click again to stop, spinner during processing, transcript appended to the textarea (ui.displayTranscription, 502-515).

Status/feedback is generally clear and uses an aria-live="polite" region (index.html:157). Theme switching is solid: an inline pre-paint script (index.html 7-21) prevents FOUC, themeMode (auto/light/dark) persists in localStorage, and the system-theme media query is observed live (ui.js loadTheme 375-386). Responsive behavior exists: a single @media (max-width:600px) block (styles.css 893-982) overlays the sidebar on mobile, shrinks the mic to 72px, and a global rule enforces 44px min touch targets (styles.css 985-990).

Transcript handling is minimal: the only transcript action is a single "Cut" button (index.html 139-145) that copies to clipboard then clears the textarea (ui.js 145-173). There is NO plain Copy, NO Save/download, and NO history. The transcript is held only in the textarea's .value and is never written to localStorage, so a reload or accidental close loses all transcribed text. Re-recording does not replace prior text: displayTranscription appends new text with "\n\n" separator (ui.js 503-507), accumulating sessions in one textarea with no per-segment structure, timestamps, or undo.

evidence.detail: grabTextButton handler is the ONLY transcript action: it calls navigator.clipboard.writeText(text) then sets transcriptElement.value='' on success. This is a destructive cut with no separate non-destructive Copy and no confirmation/undo. If the clipboard write succeeds but the user did not intend to clear, the transcript is gone (it is never persisted).

The transcript header exposes a single button labeled 'Cut' with aria-label 'Cut text to clipboard'. The transcript is a plain <textarea id='transcript' placeholder='Transcription will appear here...'>. No Copy, Save, Download, or Clear-only controls exist in markup.

displayTranscription appends to existing content: if transcriptElement.value is truthy it does value += '\n\n' + text. Recording again therefore concatenates onto prior transcriptions with only a blank-line separator — no timestamps, no segment boundaries, no way to clear just the previous result. Empty/failed API responses fall back to literal string 'No transcription returned' which is then injected as transcript text.

checkInitialSettings: when apiKey or uri missing, a 500ms setTimeout shows MESSAGES.CONFIGURE_AZURE and auto-opens the settings modal. This is the entire first-run onboarding — no welcome copy, no link to Azure docs, no explanation of which model to pick or where to get a URI/key beyond the small <small> hints in the modal (index.html:223, 236).

handleErrorState builds the user-facing error as `${ERROR_PREFIX}${errorMessage}. ${TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY}` => e.g. 'Error: <raw>. Tap mic to retry'. errorMessage is the raw thrown message (api/network). Users see raw technical strings rather than curated guidance. There is also a duplicate retry affordance: the status says 'Tap mic to retry' while a dedicated retry-button is simultaneously shown (ui.js 240), creating two competing recovery paths with different semantics (mic = re-record, retry-button = resend same audio).

Status element uses aria-live='polite' — good for routine status. However error states are routed through the same polite region (ui.js showError -> setStatus), so screen-reader users may not be promptly alerted to failures; an assertive live region or role='alert' is not used for errors.

Only one mobile breakpoint at max-width:600px. It overlays the sidebar and resizes controls but does NOT reflow the control-buttons-container; pause/mic/retry/cancel stay in a single horizontal row (gap reduced to 1rem at line 954). On very narrow phones four 44-72px circular targets plus gaps can crowd. Tablet/landscape widths (601-900px) get desktop layout with no intermediate treatment.

Theme handling is robust: inline IIFE applies dark-theme + color-scheme before first paint to avoid FOUC; applyTheme cycles auto->dark->light, persists themeMode, swaps moon/sun icons, and repaints the visualizer canvas. loadTheme attaches a prefers-color-scheme change listener that only re-applies when mode==='auto'. This is a strong, low-friction theme UX.

The control cluster renders pause-button and cancel-button with NO inline display:none (only retry-button is hidden by default at line 179). So in idle/ready state the user sees fully-styled, hover-reactive Pause and Cancel circular buttons that do nothing: state-machine guards canPause()/canCancel() (recording-state-machine.js 341-379) return false when idle, so clicks are silently inert. This is a clarity problem — controls appear actionable but aren't.

checkRecordingPrerequisites calls setStatus(DEFAULT_RESET_STATUS) ('Click the microphone to start recording') whenever prerequisites pass, and it is re-invoked on SETTINGS_LOADED / SETTINGS_SAVED / SETTINGS_UPDATED / PERMISSION_GRANTED. saveSettings emits SETTINGS_SAVED+SETTINGS_LOADED+SETTINGS_UPDATED, so opening settings mid-session and saving will reset the status line back to the idle prompt.

README advertises 'real-time' transcription and 'Live Audio Visualization' and lists 'Transcript Management: Cut transcription text to clipboard' as the only transcript feature — confirming the product intent is fire-and-forget cut-to-clipboard with no persistence/history by design. The visualizer is live but transcription is batch (sent after stop, audio-handler.js processAndSendAudio 306-323), so 'real-time transcription' overstates behavior.

risks: Data-loss risk: the transcript lives only in the textarea and is never persisted; a reload, tab close, or the destructive 'Cut' button wipes all transcribed text with no undo. For a transcription tool this is the single biggest UX liability.

Re-recording silently appends to prior text with only a blank line between sessions (ui.js 503-507); over a long session the textarea becomes an undifferentiated wall of text with no timestamps or segment separation, and there is no 'clear all' control to start fresh.

Two overlapping error-recovery affordances (status says 'Tap mic to retry' = re-record fresh audio, while the retry-button resends the same blob) can confuse users about which action preserves their last recording.
Pause and Cancel buttons are always visible and styled as interactive even when idle (index.html 160-189), so they read as available actions but are silently inert until recording starts.
Raw error strings (api/network exception messages) are surfaced directly to users via the status line (state-machine 275), which is not friendly and can leak internal detail.
Onboarding is thin: first run just opens the modal after 500ms with terse helper text; no guidance on obtaining the Azure URI/key or choosing Whisper vs MAI-Transcribe.

bugsFound.title: Destructive 'Cut' with no Copy and no persistence risks losing all transcribed text
bugsFound.location: grabTextButton handler, lines 145-173; transcript never persisted (no localStorage write anywhere for ID.TRANSCRIPT)
bugsFound.description: The only transcript action clears the textarea after copying (value=''). The transcript is never saved to localStorage, so the textarea is the sole source of truth. If the user clicks Cut intending only to copy, or the clipboard succeeds unexpectedly, the text is irrecoverably deleted. A page reload/crash likewise loses everything. There is no plain Copy, no Save/Download, no history, and no undo. For a transcription product this is a core workflow gap, not a cosmetic one.

bugsFound.title: Re-recording appends to prior transcription with no separation or clear-all
bugsFound.description: On each completed transcription, if the textarea already has content the new text is appended via value += '\n\n' + text. Multiple recordings accumulate into one undifferentiated block with no timestamps, headings, or per-segment boundaries, and the UI offers no 'clear only' control (only the destructive Cut). Users cannot easily discard one bad result without losing all, nor tell where one recording ended and the next began.

bugsFound.title: Pause and Cancel controls are always visible but inert when idle
bugsFound.location: control-buttons-container, lines 160-189; guards in recording-state-machine.js canPause 341-343 / canCancel 377-379
bugsFound.description: pause-button and cancel-button have no default display:none (only retry-button is hidden). In idle/ready state they render as fully styled, hover-animated circular buttons (styles.css .icon-btn:hover scales them up), yet clicking does nothing because the state machine guards reject the transition. Controls that look actionable but silently do nothing are a clarity/affordance defect, especially for first-time users who may think the app is broken.

bugsFound.title: Raw exception messages shown directly to users in the status line
bugsFound.description: The error status is `Error: ${errorMessage}. Tap mic to retry`, where errorMessage is the raw thrown message from the API client / fetch (e.g. HTTP status text or 'Failed to fetch'). These technical strings are not user-curated and can be confusing or leak internal detail. There is no mapping from error category to friendly guidance beyond the appended network hint.

improvementOptions.title: Persist transcript to localStorage and restore on load
improvementOptions.description: On displayTranscription and on textarea input, write transcriptElement.value to a STORAGE_KEYS.TRANSCRIPT entry; restore it in UI.init. Small, framework-free change that eliminates the data-loss-on-reload risk. Pair with a one-line privacy note (README already says no recordings stored; clarify that transcript text is cached locally).

improvementOptions.title: Add a non-destructive Copy button alongside Cut, and a separate Clear
improvementOptions.description: Split the current single 'Cut' control into Copy (clipboard only, keep text) + Clear (with confirm). Keep Cut if desired. This removes the silent data-loss path and matches user mental models. Pure HTML/CSS/JS in index.html + ui.js using the existing event-bus status messages.

improvementOptions.title: Give each new transcription a visible boundary / timestamp when appending
improvementOptions.description: In displayTranscription, when appending, prefix new text with a lightweight separator like a timestamped line (HH:MM) so accumulated sessions are scannable. Optional 'New transcript' clear action lets users reset. Keeps the single-textarea model but removes the wall-of-text problem.

improvementOptions.title: Hide Pause/Cancel until recording is active
improvementOptions.description: Default pause-button and cancel-button to display:none in index.html (as retry-button already is) and toggle them in the RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED handler (ui.js 200-246) for recording/paused states only. Removes the inert-but-clickable affordance and declutters the idle screen.

improvementOptions.title: Map common errors to friendly, actionable copy and use an assertive live region for errors
improvementOptions.description: In sendToAzureAPI/handleErrorState, translate known cases (401/403 -> 'Check your API key in settings', 404 -> 'Check your Target URI', network -> existing hint) instead of surfacing raw messages, and route errors through a role='alert'/aria-live='assertive' region so screen readers are notified. Reuses existing MESSAGES constants.

improvementOptions.title: Strengthen first-run onboarding inside the existing modal
improvementOptions.description: Add concise guidance to the settings modal (e.g. a short 'Where do I get these?' expandable with the Azure portal path, and a sentence explaining Whisper vs MAI-Transcribe). No new dependency — just markup + copy. Reduces the abrupt 'modal pops open with two cryptic fields' first impression.

improvementOptions.title: Add an intermediate tablet/landscape breakpoint and reflow controls on very narrow screens
improvementOptions.description: The single 600px breakpoint leaves 601-900px on desktop layout and keeps four circular controls in one row on small phones. Add a tablet treatment and allow the control cluster to wrap/shrink gracefully below ~360px.

review.currentState: The recording lifecycle is driven by an 8-state FSM (RecordingStateMachine in js/recording-state-machine.js) validated against STATE_TRANSITIONS in js/constants.js (lines 352-361). AudioHandler (js/audio-handler.js) owns the MediaRecorder, stream, audioChunks, and timer interval, and drives transitions; UI (js/ui.js) reacts to RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED and discrete UI_* events. The happy paths (start → record → pause/resume → stop → process → idle, and cancel → idle) are coherent and well covered by tests/recording-integration.vitest.js. Transitions are validated before mutation and invalid ones are rejected with a logged error (transitionTo, lines 86-111). The recently added retry flow (retryPendingTranscription, lines 325-346; commit f1504e3) preserves the failed audio blob in pendingRetryBlob and re-sends it, but it transitions ERROR→IDLE→PROCESSING, and IDLE→PROCESSING is NOT a legal transition in the matrix, so the retry path is broken at the state-machine level. Pause/cancel/retry buttons are always present and enabled in the DOM (index.html lines 161-189; never disabled/hidden by state in ui.js), so guards live entirely in AudioHandler capability checks. emit() is fully synchronous (event-bus.js lines 100-135), which keeps most ordering deterministic, but state handlers are async and several callers ignore the boolean returned by transitionTo.

evidence.detail: STATE_TRANSITIONS: IDLE maps to [INITIALIZING, ERROR] only. There is no IDLE→PROCESSING edge. The retry flow depends on exactly that edge.

retryPendingTranscription does: await transitionTo(IDLE); await transitionTo(PROCESSING); await sendToAzureAPI(retryBlob). The PROCESSING transition is from IDLE, which canTransitionTo() rejects, so it returns false (state stays IDLE) yet the code ignores the return and calls the API anyway.

transitionTo() on invalid transition calls errorHandler.handleError(new Error('Invalid state transition from idle to processing')) and returns false without changing state or running handleProcessingState.
handleError emits APP_EVENTS.ERROR_OCCURRED with the raw error message.
UI listens to ERROR_OCCURRED and calls showError(message), so the raw 'Invalid state transition from idle to processing' string is surfaced to the user during retry.

The retry test only asserts final state === IDLE and pendingRetryBlob === null. Because both failed transitions (IDLE→PROCESSING and the later IDLE→IDLE) leave the state at IDLE, the test passes by accident and never verifies that PROCESSING was entered or the spinner shown — masking the bug.

handleProcessingState (in state machine) is what emits API_REQUEST_START, UI_SPINNER_SHOW, and UI_BUTTON_DISABLE_MIC. Since the PROCESSING transition fails during retry, none of these fire: no spinner, and the mic button stays enabled.
stopRecordingFlow ignores the return of transitionTo(STOPPING) and always runs clearInterval + UI_TIMER_RESET + safeStopRecorder.

canInvokeStop() returns true for STOPPING and CANCELLING, so toggleRecording (audio-handler.js:79-81) re-enters stopRecordingFlow on a repeat mic click while already stopping; transitionTo(STOPPING) from STOPPING is invalid and logs an ERROR_OCCURRED, surfacing a spurious error to the user, plus a redundant safeStopRecorder() call.

MediaRecorder 'stop' handler branches on getState()===CANCELLING to stop tracks + go IDLE; otherwise transitions to PROCESSING and processes audio. If stop() never fires its event (e.g. recorder already inactive when cancel arrives), CANCELLING only allows →IDLE and there is no fallback, so the stream tracks (stopped only inside the stop handler) leak and the state is stuck in CANCELLING.

togglePause guards on canPause()/canResume() before dereferencing this.mediaRecorder, so the always-clickable pause button is safe; resume() is treated as synchronous (state→RECORDING immediately after mediaRecorder.resume()), which can desync if resume rejects.

pause-button and cancel-button have no display:none and are never disabled in JS; only the retry button starts hidden/disabled. So pause/cancel are clickable in every state and rely solely on AudioHandler capability guards.

startRecordingFlow correctly recovers ERROR→IDLE then clears pendingRetryBlob before INITIALIZING, and routes browser/config failures back to IDLE. This path is solid; the retry path is the regressed sibling.

risks: The headline retry flow added in commit f1504e3 is functionally broken at the FSM layer: clicking Retry attempts an illegal IDLE→PROCESSING transition, so no spinner shows, the mic stays enabled (allowing a concurrent new recording to start mid-retry), and a raw 'Invalid state transition' string is shown to the user. The transcription itself may still complete, making the breakage intermittent and confusing.
The existing retry test passes by coincidence (both broken transitions land on IDLE), giving false confidence that the retry path is correct.
Re-clicking the mic button during STOPPING/CANCELLING (allowed by canInvokeStop) produces user-visible 'Invalid state transition' error messages and redundant stop calls.

Cancel relies entirely on the MediaRecorder 'stop' event firing to stop stream tracks and leave CANCELLING; if that event never fires the app is stuck in CANCELLING with a leaked mic stream and no recovery path.

Because pause/cancel buttons are always enabled in the DOM, correctness depends 100% on AudioHandler guards rather than UI affordances; any future caller that bypasses those guards re-opens illegal-transition risk.

bugsFound.title: Retry flow uses an illegal IDLE→PROCESSING transition; retry runs with no spinner, mic left enabled, and shows a raw 'Invalid state transition' error
bugsFound.description: retryPendingTranscription transitions ERROR→IDLE (valid) then IDLE→PROCESSING. STATE_TRANSITIONS[IDLE] is [INITIALIZING, ERROR] (constants.js:353), so canTransitionTo(PROCESSING) returns false. transitionTo (recording-state-machine.js:86-91) then calls errorHandler.handleError with Error('Invalid state transition from idle to processing'), which emits ERROR_OCCURRED → UI.showError (ui.js:259-262), flashing that raw string to the user. It returns false and never runs handleProcessingState, so API_REQUEST_START / UI_SPINNER_SHOW / UI_BUTTON_DISABLE_MIC never fire (recording-state-machine.js:228-237). The code ignores the false return and calls sendToAzureAPI anyway: the API request runs while the FSM is still IDLE and the mic button is still enabled, so a user can click the mic and start a fresh recording (startRecordingFlow allowed because canRecord() is true in IDLE) concurrently with the in-flight retry. On success the subsequent transitionTo(IDLE) is also an illegal IDLE→IDLE (line 338) and silently fails; the state only ends at IDLE by coincidence, which is exactly why the test at tests/audio-handler-integration.vitest.js:313 passes despite the bug. Correct fix: transition ERROR→PROCESSING is already legal (constants.js:358 lists PROCESSING from STOPPING and ERROR has only [IDLE]); the minimal fix is to add PROCESSING to STATE_TRANSITIONS[ERROR] and go straight ERROR→PROCESSING (dropping the IDLE hop), or add an explicit ERROR→PROCESSING/IDLE→PROCESSING edge — and check the boolean return before sending.

bugsFound.title: Re-clicking mic while STOPPING/CANCELLING triggers spurious 'Invalid state transition' errors and redundant stop
bugsFound.location: toggleRecording lines 76-82 + stopRecordingFlow lines 158-171; canInvokeStop in recording-state-machine.js:362-369
bugsFound.description: canInvokeStop() returns true for STOPPING and CANCELLING, so a second mic click during teardown calls stopRecordingFlow again. transitionTo(STOPPING) is invalid from STOPPING and from CANCELLING (constants.js:357,359), so it logs ERROR_OCCURRED (surfaced to the user via ui.js:259-262) and returns false, but stopRecordingFlow ignores the return and still runs clearInterval, emits a redundant UI_TIMER_RESET, and calls safeStopRecorder() again. The actual double mediaRecorder.stop() is prevented by the state!=='inactive' guard (audio-handler.js:222-229), so no crash, but the user sees a confusing raw error string. Fix: guard stopRecordingFlow on the transitionTo return value, or remove STOPPING/CANCELLING from canInvokeStop and treat repeat clicks as no-ops.

bugsFound.title: Cancel can leave a stuck CANCELLING state and leaked mic stream if the MediaRecorder 'stop' event never fires
bugsFound.location: cancelRecording lines 262-267, stop handler lines 191-208; CANCELLING transitions in constants.js:359
bugsFound.description: All cleanup for the cancel path (stream.getTracks().forEach(t=>t.stop()), transition to IDLE, cleanup()) lives inside the MediaRecorder 'stop' event handler (lines 195-200). safeStopRecorder only calls stop() when state!=='inactive'. If the recorder is already inactive or errors without emitting 'stop' (e.g. a track ended / device unplugged mid-recording), the handler never runs: state is stuck in CANCELLING (whose only legal exit is IDLE, constants.js:359) and the getUserMedia stream is never stopped, leaking the microphone (browser recording indicator stays on). There is no timeout/fallback to force IDLE. Not proven against a real browser, but the single-exit design is fragile. Fix: stop stream tracks in cancelRecording itself (not only in the stop handler) and/or add a fallback transition to IDLE.

bugsFound.title: Resume treated as synchronous; state→RECORDING set before mediaRecorder.resume() is confirmed
bugsFound.description: On resume, the code calls this.mediaRecorder.resume(), starts the timer, and immediately transitions PAUSED→RECORDING. resume() can throw/reject in some browsers if the recorder is no longer in 'paused' state; because the transition is not gated on a 'resume' event, the FSM can report RECORDING while the underlying recorder is not actually capturing, leaving a silent dead recording. Lower risk because the guard canResume() requires PAUSED, but there is no verification that resume actually took effect.

improvementOptions.title: Fix the retry transition path (ERROR→PROCESSING) and respect transitionTo return values
improvementOptions.description: Make retryPendingTranscription use a legal transition: add PROCESSING to STATE_TRANSITIONS[ERROR] in js/constants.js (and/or IDLE→PROCESSING) and transition ERROR→PROCESSING directly instead of ERROR→IDLE→PROCESSING; then guard the subsequent sendToAzureAPI on the boolean return of transitionTo so the API is never called when the FSM rejected the transition. Likewise change the success branch to a legal PROCESSING→IDLE instead of IDLE→IDLE. This restores the spinner, disables the mic during retry, and stops the spurious 'Invalid state transition' message.

improvementOptions.title: Strengthen retry test to assert the PROCESSING phase and absence of invalid-transition errors
improvementOptions.description: Update tests/audio-handler-integration.vitest.js retry tests to assert that RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED fires with newState PROCESSING, that UI_SPINNER_SHOW / UI_BUTTON_DISABLE_MIC fire during retry, and that ERROR_OCCURRED is NOT emitted with an 'Invalid state transition' message. The current test asserts only final state IDLE and pendingRetryBlob null, which both happen to hold even with the broken matrix.

improvementOptions.title: Make stopRecordingFlow and toggleRecording honor transition results
improvementOptions.description: In stopRecordingFlow, only proceed with clearInterval/UI_TIMER_RESET/safeStopRecorder when transitionTo(STOPPING) returns true, and either remove STOPPING/CANCELLING from canInvokeStop or short-circuit repeat clicks, so a second mic press during teardown is a clean no-op rather than a user-visible error.

improvementOptions.title: Centralize stream-track teardown so cancel cannot leak the mic
improvementOptions.description: Move stream.getTracks().forEach(t=>t.stop()) out of the MediaRecorder 'stop' handler into a single cleanup point invoked by both the normal and cancel paths (and add a fallback CANCELLING→IDLE if the stop event does not arrive within a short timeout), eliminating the stuck-CANCELLING / leaked-microphone failure mode.

improvementOptions.title: Reflect FSM capabilities in the pause/cancel button affordances
improvementOptions.description: Disable or hide pause/cancel buttons outside RECORDING/PAUSED (they are currently always enabled per index.html:161-189 and ui.js) so illegal operations are prevented at the UI layer as well as the guard layer, reducing reliance on AudioHandler capability checks and avoiding confusing no-op clicks.

review.currentState:
The codebase has a partial, well-intentioned adapter abstraction that is real but shallow. Adapters live in js/model-adapters/{whisper,whisper-translate,mai-transcribe}.js and are registered in a Map in js/model-adapters/index.js. The adapter contract is duck-typed: each adapter object exposes `id`, `label`, `storageKeys: {apiKey, uri}`, `async buildRequest(audioBlob, config, onProgress) -> {headers, body, statusMessage}`, and `parseResponse(data) -> string`. AzureAPIClient.transcribe() (js/api-client.js:49-91) correctly delegates request-building and parsing to whichever adapter `_getModelAdapter(config.model)` resolves, and injects the registry via constructor (api-client.js:32), so the request/response layer is genuinely open to new providers and is unit-tested with a synthetic `fake-model` adapter (tests/model-adapters.vitest.js:104-152).

However, the abstraction does not extend past the request/response boundary. Two hard Azure/whisper-vs-mai assumptions leak into shared, non-adapter code: (1) js/settings.js hard-codes an `isMai` ternary in at least six places (getModelConfig:684-688, _getActiveInputs:498-502, getValidationErrors:550-557, saveSettings:599-612, updateSettingsVisibility:415, and the modal-visibility toggles), so credential storage, retrieval, form-field routing, and validation all branch on exactly two model families rather than reading the adapter's own `storageKeys`. (2) index.html hard-codes the model dropdown options (lines 78-79 and 211-212) plus dedicated `whisper-settings` / `mai-transcribe-settings` DOM blocks. The adapter's `storageKeys` and `label` fields are declared but never read by any consumer (verified by grep across js/ and index.html) — they are dead metadata that signals intended-but-unfinished registry-driven config.

Retry/timeout logic lives in the right place (centralized in api-client.js _fetchWithRetry:137-162, RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES/backoff schedule:11-14) but is Azure-shaped: it only honors HTTP-status retries and the `Retry-After` header, and there is no request timeout or AbortController at all — fetch options (api-client.js:64-68) carry only method/headers/body with no `signal`. Streaming is neither supported nor structurally possible: the contract requires buildRequest to return a buffered body and parseResponse to take a fully-read string/JSON (api-client.js:74-78), with no token/partial-result channel.

evidence.detail: transcribe() resolves an adapter by model id, calls adapter.buildRequest(audioBlob, config, onProgress) for {headers, body, statusMessage}, fetches config.uri, then adapter.parseResponse(data). This is the clean part of the contract and is provider-agnostic at the request/response boundary.
_getModelAdapter(model) does registry.get(model) and throws 'Unsupported transcription model' if missing. The registry is the single routing point — open/closed at this layer.

Adapter shape: {id, label, storageKeys:{apiKey,uri}, async buildRequest()->{headers,body,statusMessage}, parseResponse}. This is the de-facto interface; it is never declared as a type/JSDoc typedef or documented in the spec.

Registry is a Map literal; comment explicitly warns 'Order matters for AzureAPIClient.parseResponse(): adapters are tried in legacy parse precedence.' Adding a 4th adapter requires understanding this ordering coupling.

getModelConfig() hard-codes const isMai = model === MODEL_TYPES.MAI_TRANSCRIBE and picks storage keys via ternary. A 4th provider's credentials cannot be stored/retrieved without editing this method — the adapter's own storageKeys field is ignored.

_getActiveInputs(), getValidationErrors(), and saveSettings() all branch on isMai for DOM input selection, key-format validation (32-char hex for whisper vs longer for speech), and which localStorage keys to write. Three more edit sites per new provider.

Both model <select> menus and the per-model settings <div> blocks are hard-coded HTML. whisper-translate is registered as an adapter (model-adapters/index.js:7) and has a MODEL_TYPES entry (constants.js:94) but appears in NEITHER dropdown, so it is unreachable from the UI despite being fully wired and tested.

Public parseResponse() loops over ALL registered adapters' parseResponse in registry order and returns the first that does not throw. Because parseWhisperResponse and parseMaiTranscribeResponse both accept {text}, the result depends on registry insertion order, not the active model.

fetch is called with only {method, headers, body}; no signal/AbortController and no overall timeout. Retry is status-code + Retry-After driven only (RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES:12). A hung connection or a provider that signals overload differently will not be handled.

parseWhisperResponse and parseMaiTranscribeResponse are buffered-only (string or parsed JSON in, string out). No streaming/partial contract exists; combinedPhrases vs text shapes are the only handled cases.

Spec is named/scoped to Azure ('AzureAPIClient', REQ-002 enumerates exactly the three Azure models, REQ-006 hard-codes the two Azure auth headers). It mentions extensibility only aspirationally (line 168: 'adding a new transcription service would involve creating a new client') and does NOT document the adapter interface, storageKeys, or how to register a provider.

risks: Adding a 4th provider (OpenAI/Deepgram/local) touches at least 5-6 files: a new adapter, registry registration, getModelConfig, _getActiveInputs/getValidationErrors/saveSettings in settings.js, the two index.html dropdowns, and a new per-model settings DOM block. The adapter abstraction hides only ~30% of the work; the credential/UI plumbing is still per-model copy-paste.

Settings credential storage is keyed by a hard-coded isMai split into exactly two key pairs (WHISPER_* and MAI_TRANSCRIBE_*). A third credential family has nowhere to live without new STORAGE_KEYS and new branches; the adapter.storageKeys field that should drive this is dead code.

The key-format validation in getValidationErrors (settings.js:555, 32-char hex regex for non-MAI) is an Azure-Whisper-specific assumption applied to every non-MAI model. A future OpenAI/Deepgram key would be wrongly rejected as 'Invalid API key format'.

Public parseResponse() returning the first non-throwing parser in registry order (api-client.js:247-253) means a new provider whose response shape overlaps {text} could be silently mis-parsed depending on Map insertion order. The transcribe() path is strict (uses the active adapter), but the public helper is order-fragile.
No request timeout/AbortController means a stalled provider connection hangs the PROCESSING state indefinitely with no recovery path.
whisper-translate being registered/tested but absent from both dropdowns shows the UI and adapter registry have already drifted out of sync — evidence the manual two-place wiring is error-prone.

bugsFound.title: whisper-translate adapter is registered and tested but unreachable from the UI
bugsFound.description: whisperTranslateModelAdapter is imported and registered in the modelAdapterRegistry (index.js:7,13), has a MODEL_TYPES.WHISPER_TRANSLATE constant (constants.js:94), and has full passing tests (tests/model-adapters.vitest.js:210-230). But neither the main model-select (index.html:77-80) nor the settings-model-select (index.html:210-213) contains a 'whisper-translate' <option>. There is no other code path that sets model-select.value to 'whisper-translate'. Therefore a user can never select translate mode; the feature is dead at runtime. Failure scenario: any user expecting translation gets only transcription, and the maintained+tested adapter gives a false sense the feature ships.

bugsFound.title: Public parseResponse() result depends on registry insertion order, not the active model
bugsFound.description: parseResponse(data) iterates this.adapterRegistry.values() and returns the first adapter whose parseResponse does not throw. Both parseWhisperResponse and parseMaiTranscribeResponse accept a JSON object with a `text` field, and parseMaiTranscribeResponse additionally handles combinedPhrases. Because the registry Map is ordered [mai, whisper, whisper-translate] (index.js:11-13), a response containing BOTH combinedPhrases and text is always parsed as MAI regardless of which model actually made the request (this exact behavior is asserted in tests/model-adapters.vitest.js:188-196). transcribe() itself is safe (it uses the active adapter at api-client.js:78), but any external caller of the public parseResponse gets order-coupled, model-agnostic parsing. Adding a 4th provider whose shape overlaps {text} could be silently mis-parsed depending on where it is inserted in the Map. The index.js comment ('Order matters...') acknowledges this as load-bearing coupling rather than fixing it.

bugsFound.title: No request timeout or AbortController on transcription fetch
bugsFound.description: The fetch call is built with only {method, headers, body} (lines 64-68) and _fetchWithRetry awaits fetch(uri, options) with no signal. Retry only triggers on RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES responses (line 141); a connection that opens but never responds (no status code) is never timed out or aborted. The PROCESSING state would hang indefinitely. This is provider-agnostic infrastructure that belongs in the client, so it is the right place to fix, but it is currently absent.

improvementOptions.title: Make settings credential storage adapter-driven via the existing storageKeys field
improvementOptions.description: settings.js getModelConfig (682-690), _getActiveInputs (497-503), getValidationErrors (543-575), and saveSettings (599-612) all hard-code a two-way isMai branch. Drive these from the active adapter's already-declared storageKeys.{apiKey,uri} (pulled from modelAdapterRegistry.get(model)) instead. This deletes the dead-code status of storageKeys and removes the hard requirement to edit settings.js per provider. Keep it incremental: keep the existing localStorage key names so current users' saved credentials still load. This is the single highest-leverage change for extensibility.

improvementOptions.title: Generate model dropdown options and per-model settings sections from the registry
improvementOptions.description: Replace the hard-coded <option> lists (index.html:78-79,211-212) and the static whisper-settings/mai-transcribe-settings <div>s with markup generated from modelAdapterRegistry entries using each adapter's id + label (label is currently dead metadata). This fixes the whisper-translate-unreachable bug for free and removes the two-place dropdown drift that already caused that bug. No framework needed — a small DOM-builder in settings.js fits the no-build constraint.

improvementOptions.title: Document and formalize the adapter contract
improvementOptions.description: The interface ({id, label, storageKeys, buildRequest, parseResponse}) exists only implicitly. Add a JSDoc @typedef ModelAdapter and a short 'Adding a provider' section to spec/spec-design-api-client.md (which currently only aspirationally mentions extensibility at line 168 and is otherwise Azure-scoped). Cite required fields, the buildRequest return shape, that parseResponse must throw on unrecognized shapes, and the registry-order caveat. Low effort, high payoff for a 4th-provider contributor.

improvementOptions.title: Move per-model key-format validation into the adapter
improvementOptions.description: getValidationErrors (settings.js:555) applies an Azure-Whisper-specific 32-char-hex regex to every non-MAI model. Add an optional validateApiKey(key) hook to the adapter contract and call it from getValidationErrors so each provider owns its key rules. Prevents a future OpenAI/Deepgram key from being wrongly rejected. Falls back to the generic API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN when an adapter omits the hook.

improvementOptions.title: Add an AbortController-based request timeout in the API client
improvementOptions.description: Thread an AbortController signal into the fetch options in _fetchWithRetry (api-client.js:137-162) with a configurable overall timeout, and treat abort as a retryable/terminal error with a clear message. Provider-agnostic, belongs in the client, and closes the indefinite-hang gap. Keep the existing status-code retry behavior intact.

improvementOptions.title: Decide and document streaming posture
improvementOptions.description: The current contract is buffered-only (buildRequest returns a complete body, parseResponse takes a fully-read response). True streaming (e.g. Deepgram/OpenAI realtime over WebSocket) cannot be added without a second contract method (e.g. an optional async-generator transcribeStream). For 2.0, the pragmatic call is to explicitly document that the adapter contract is request/response only and that streaming providers are out of scope, rather than half-building it. Revisit only if a streaming provider becomes a real requirement.

review.currentState: All settings persist as flat, unversioned localStorage string entries. The keys are centralized in STORAGE_KEYS (js/constants.js:37-47): transcription_model, whisper_uri, whisper_api_key, mai_transcribe_uri, mai_transcribe_api_key, themeMode, recording_environment, input_device, sidebar_pinned. js/settings.js owns reads/writes for everything except themeMode (owned by js/ui.js:103-389). Credentials (API keys) are stored in plaintext in localStorage (settings.js:608/611) and read back verbatim by getModelConfig (settings.js:682-690), which is the single source consumed by api-client.validateConfig (api-client.js:266) and audio-handler (audio-handler.js:116). Per-model config is keyed by model id: getModelConfig and saveSettings branch on isMai to pick the whisper_* or mai_transcribe_* key pair; the three model adapters (whisper.js, whisper-translate.js, mai-transcribe.js) each declare a storageKeys map, with whisper and whisper-translate intentionally sharing WHISPER_API_KEY/WHISPER_URI. Validation lives in getValidationErrors (settings.js:543-575): it requires a non-empty key matching API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN (visible ASCII, no whitespace), enforces a strict 32-char hex format for non-MAI keys (settings.js:555), requires a URI that parses as a URL and uses https: (settings.js:566). Save flow (saveSettings, settings.js:585-650) sanitizes, validates via getValidationErrors, writes to localStorage, and emits SETTINGS_SAVED/SETTINGS_LOADED/SETTINGS_UPDATED. Settings is heavily coupled to the DOM: the constructor caches ~20 getElementById references (settings.js:36-62) and the same class also owns the entire side-panel/hover/device-list UI (lines 216-405). There is NO migration or schema-versioning layer and NO try/catch around any localStorage call. Coverage 83.89%/77.45% branch maps directly onto untested device/noise/sidebar/whisper-translate paths.

evidence.detail: saveSettings persists plaintext API key and URI to localStorage with no try/catch. localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEYS.WHISPER_API_KEY, apiKey) etc. A QuotaExceededError or a Safari private-mode SecurityError would throw uncaught, after the model key (line 605) is already written, leaving a torn write (model switched but credentials not saved).

getValidationErrors is the only validation actually invoked on save (settings.js:588). validateConfiguration (settings.js:527-534), which emits SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR, is never called anywhere in js/ (grep shows only its own definition and tests). So the public-looking validateConfiguration API is dead in production.

Whisper keys are rejected unless they match /^[A-F0-9]{32}$/i. Azure OpenAI Whisper resource keys ARE 32-char hex, but this hard-codes an Azure key-format assumption into validation; any future key rotation to a different length/charset silently blocks save. MAI keys get no length/format check at all beyond API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN — asymmetric validation.

loadSettingsToForm only assigns input.value when the stored value is truthy (if (this.whisperKeyInput && whisperKey)). When a model has no saved key, the input retains whatever was there before, so opening the modal after switching models can display a stale/other-model key in the field.

MODEL_TYPES.WHISPER_TRANSLATE ('whisper-translate') and whisperTranslateModelAdapter are registered (model-adapters/index.js:13) but neither model-select nor settings-model-select offers it as an <option>. It is unreachable through the UI and only persistable by hand-editing localStorage.

Settings constructor resolves ~20 DOM nodes and the class mixes credential/config persistence with side-panel hover state, device enumeration (populateDeviceList, 378-405) and sidebar pinning. Storage logic is not separable from DOM; no storage abstraction layer exists.
themeMode is read/written directly by ui.js using STORAGE_KEYS.THEME_MODE, bypassing Settings entirely. Persistence ownership is split across two modules with no single storage gateway.

grep for populateDeviceList, getAvailableDevices, loadNoiseToggle, DEVICE_CHANGED, INPUT_DEVICE, pinSidebar, SIDEBAR_PINNED returns NOTHING in the unit/persistence suites. The noise-toggle live-save (settings.js:183-193), input-device live-save+removeItem (196-206), sidebar pin/unpin persistence (363/371), loadNoiseToggle (86-91) and populateDeviceList are untested — this is the bulk of the 77.45% branch gap. settings-sidebar.vitest.js only tests hover transition mechanics, not persistence.

risks: API keys stored in plaintext localStorage are readable by any XSS payload or browser extension; there is no encryption, sessionStorage option, or expiry. Acceptable for a personal tool but a clear 2.0 documentation/UX honesty item.

No try/catch on any localStorage access: Safari private mode (SecurityError) and quota exhaustion throw uncaught, and saveSettings can produce a torn write (model key updated, credentials not) because setItem(MODEL) precedes the credential writes.
No schema versioning: future renames of any STORAGE_KEYS value silently orphan existing users' stored credentials with no migration path, forcing manual reconfiguration.

Validation logic is duplicated and divergent between settings.js (32-hex Whisper rule, https-only) and api-client.validateConfig (only API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN + URL-parseable, no https/hex check). A value rejected by the modal could still be accepted by the API path if written directly, and vice versa.
Settings class conflates credential storage, validation, and side-panel/device UI, making the storage layer untestable in isolation and inflating the file to a single 700+ line god-object.

bugsFound.title: Unhandled localStorage exceptions cause torn writes in saveSettings
bugsFound.description: saveSettings calls localStorage.setItem with no try/catch. The model key is written first (line 605: setItem(STORAGE_KEYS.MODEL, currentModel)), then the credential pair (lines 607-611). In Safari private browsing setItem throws SecurityError, and a full storage throws QuotaExceededError. If the throw happens on the credential write, the model has already been switched but the key/URI were not saved, leaving an inconsistent persisted state; the success status/SETTINGS_SAVED events (620-650) also never fire, yet the modal-close at 618 also never runs, so the user sees a frozen modal with a half-applied model switch. No code path recovers from this.

bugsFound.title: loadSettingsToForm leaves stale credentials in inputs when target model has no saved value
bugsFound.description: Each input is only populated when its stored value is truthy: `if (this.whisperKeyInput && whisperKey)` and the MAI equivalents. The function never clears inputs. If the user previously typed/loaded a Whisper key, then switches the modal selector to MAI (which has no saved key) and reopens or re-renders the form, the MAI key field can retain a previously shown value rather than showing empty. Because save reads from the active model's input (_getActiveInputs, 497-503), this risks writing a stale value under the wrong model key. Confirmed by the truthy-guard pattern with no else-branch clearing.

bugsFound.title: validateConfiguration is dead code; only getValidationErrors is wired to save
bugsFound.description: validateConfiguration() emits APP_EVENTS.SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR and returns bool, but grep across js/ shows it is never invoked outside its own definition and tests. saveSettings independently calls getValidationErrors (line 588) and emits SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR itself (590). The documented public method validateConfiguration therefore never runs in production, so any consumer relying on it (or its event) for a non-save validation flow gets nothing. Maintenance hazard: two parallel validation entry points, one unused.

bugsFound.title: whisper-translate model is registered but not selectable, so its config is unreachable
bugsFound.description: whisperTranslateModelAdapter (id 'whisper-translate') is in the registry and shares WHISPER_API_KEY/WHISPER_URI, but index.html offers only 'whisper' and 'mai-transcribe' options in both #model-select (lines 77-78) and #settings-model-select (210-212). The settings layer's isMai/non-isMai branching also has no concept of translate. Result: a fully built adapter+config path is dead from the UI and only activatable by manually setting transcription_model='whisper-translate' in localStorage. Either wire it into the selectors or remove it to avoid confusing the per-model config model.

bugsFound.title: Asymmetric API-key format validation between Whisper (strict) and MAI (none)
bugsFound.description: Non-MAI keys must match /^[A-F0-9]{32}$/i (line 555) while MAI keys are only checked against API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN. This hard-codes the current Azure Whisper key shape; if Azure issues a key of different length/charset (e.g. a longer Speech key, which the comment at 556 itself acknowledges exists), the modal will reject a valid key and block the user, with no override. Classified likely because the exact future Azure key format is an external assumption, but the over-strict regex with no escape hatch is read directly from source.

improvementOptions.title: Introduce a thin storage gateway module (no new deps)
improvementOptions.description: Extract a small storage.js wrapping get/set/remove with a single try/catch that emits a user-visible status on SecurityError/QuotaExceededError and makes saveSettings transactional (write credentials before flipping the model key, or roll back on failure). Route ui.js themeMode through it too so persistence ownership is centralized. Keeps the no-build ES-module constraint; pure refactor at the right ownership boundary. Fixes the torn-write bug and the split themeMode ownership.

improvementOptions.title: Add settings schema versioning + migration hook
improvementOptions.description: Store a settings_schema_version key and a tiny migrate() run at Settings.init() before loadSavedModel. Even a no-op v1 today establishes the seam so any future STORAGE_KEYS rename (the current flat keys are brittle) can migrate existing users' plaintext credentials instead of orphaning them. Small, incremental, no dependency.

improvementOptions.title: Clear form inputs in loadSettingsToForm and add tests for it
improvementOptions.description: Set whisper/MAI key+URI inputs to '' before conditionally repopulating, eliminating the stale-credential display/write risk, and add a settings-persistence test that opens the modal for a model with no saved key after another model had a value. Directly closes a confirmed low-severity bug and one untested branch.

improvementOptions.title: Cover the device/noise/sidebar persistence branches
improvementOptions.description: Add unit tests for loadNoiseToggle (86-91), the noise-toggle and input-device change listeners (183-206, including removeItem when deviceId empty), populateDeviceList (378-405 — default-option handling, skipping 'default' pseudo-device, restoring savedDevice), and sidebar pin/unpin persistence (363/371). These are the concrete sources of the 77.45% branch / 83.89% line gap; none are touched by settings-unit or settings-persistence today.

improvementOptions.title: Reconcile the two validation paths
improvementOptions.description: Make api-client.validateConfig and settings.getValidationErrors share one validator (e.g. export a validateCredentials(model, key, uri) helper from settings or a new validation module) so https-only and key-format rules cannot diverge. Then either delete the unused validateConfiguration or make it the single entry point. Removes duplicate divergent logic.

improvementOptions.title: Decide whisper-translate: wire it in or remove it
improvementOptions.description: Either add the <option value="whisper-translate"> to both selects and teach settings' isMai branching to handle the third model, or delete whisperTranslateModelAdapter from the registry. Eliminates an unreachable per-model config path that confuses the configuration architecture.

improvementOptions.title: Soften the Whisper 32-hex key check into a warning with override
improvementOptions.description: Replace the hard reject at settings.js:555 with a non-blocking warning (or relax to a min-length/charset check) so a valid-but-differently-shaped Azure key is not permanently blocked. Low effort, removes a brittle external assumption.

improvementOptions.title: Document the plaintext-credential threat model in README
improvementOptions.description: API keys live unencrypted in localStorage (settings.js:608/611). For a personal browser tool this is a reasonable tradeoff, but 2.0 should state it explicitly (XSS/extension exposure, shared-machine risk) and recommend dedicated low-scope Azure keys. No code change; honesty/UX item.

review.currentState: The app has a reasonably mature, mostly-recoverable error model built on an event bus. Three surfacing layers exist: (1) errorHandler.handleError (js/error-handler.js:17-22) logs and emits ERROR_OCCURRED, consumed by ui.js:259-262 -> showError -> setStatus; (2) the state machine's ERROR state (recording-state-machine.js:271-280) emits UI_STATUS_UPDATE with "Error: <msg>. Tap mic to retry"; (3) PermissionManager emits both UI_STATUS_UPDATE and PERMISSION_ERROR/PERMISSION_DENIED with bespoke per-error-name messages (permission-manager.js:193-246). Recovery is genuinely good in the common cases: every terminal error funnels the state machine to ERROR, which re-enables the mic button and (since canRecord() returns true for ERROR, recording-state-machine.js:330-333) lets the user immediately re-record; startRecordingFlow first transitions ERROR->IDLE (audio-handler.js:99-101). Transcription failures preserve pendingRetryBlob so a dedicated retry button can re-send the same audio without re-recording (audio-handler.js:308, 325-346). Retry/backoff for transient HTTP (429/5xx) is well-built: capped exponential schedule [2,4,8,16,32]s, Retry-After parsing for both numeric-seconds and HTTP-date forms clamped to 60s, and the error body is drained before retry to release the connection (api-client.js:137-221). Network-offline is detected pre-flight via navigator.onLine and post-hoc via TypeError/"failed to fetch" pattern-matching, appending a "Check your internet connection" hint (audio-handler.js:350-351, 379-383). The main gaps are: no request timeout/AbortController anywhere (a hung fetch hangs PROCESSING forever); duplicated/competing error surfacing across the three layers; and several swallowed-error sites.

evidence.detail: _fetchWithRetry loops attempt 0..MAX (inclusive) and returns the response inside the loop, but has NO return statement after the for-loop. If the loop body's final iteration neither returns nor the math lines up, the function falls through to return undefined. In practice attempt===MAX_TRANSCRIPTION_RETRIES (line 141) guarantees a return on the last attempt, so it is currently safe — but it relies on that invariant with zero defensive fallback; any future change to the loop bound silently yields `undefined`, which then hits `response.ok` at api-client.js:70 and throws a confusing TypeError instead of an API error.

On any transcribe() failure, _handleApiError calls errorHandler.handleError (-> ERROR_OCCURRED -> ui.showError -> setStatus) AND emits API_REQUEST_ERROR, then the error re-throws (api-client.js:88-89) and ultimately the state machine ERROR handler emits a THIRD UI message (UI_STATUS_UPDATE 'Error: ...Tap mic to retry'). setStatus is invoked at least twice per error with different strings; because emit is synchronous and ordered (event-bus.js:100-122), the state-machine message fires last and wins, so showError's work is wasted and the two layers can diverge if ordering ever changes.

sendToAzureAPI catch calls this.cleanup() (which nulls mediaRecorder and zeroes audioChunks) but deliberately does NOT clear pendingRetryBlob, so retry survives. Good. However the catch returns {success:false} and processAndSendAudio (lines 317-322) transitions to ERROR with canRetry=Boolean(pendingRetryBlob). Since pendingRetryBlob was set at line 308 and never cleared on failure, canRetry is effectively always true for any post-recording transcription error, even for non-retryable errors like 400/401/403 (bad key, bad URI) — the retry button is shown for errors that will deterministically fail again.

stopRecordingFlow and safeStopRecorder catch a recorder.stop() failure by emitting RECORDING_ERROR directly, but there is NO listener on RECORDING_ERROR that drives the state machine to ERROR or resets the UI (grep shows RECORDING_ERROR is only emitted, never consumed to change state). If mediaRecorder.stop() throws, the machine is left in STOPPING with the spinner/timer logic already half-run and no path to recovery except the user noticing and that the mic stays clickable.

convertToWav (decodeAudioData) is awaited inside adapter.buildRequest which runs at api-client.js:54 INSIDE transcribe's try, so a decode failure (corrupt/empty/too-short WebM) is caught and surfaced as a generic error. But it is categorized like a network/API error — there is no specific message for 'audio could not be decoded', and because navigator.onLine is true, the user may see a misleading retry hint. decodeAudioData on an empty blob (zero-length recording) rejects with a vague EncodingError.

In handlePermissionError, only NotAllowedError/PermissionDeniedError emit PERMISSION_DENIED; NotFoundError, NotReadableError, OverconstrainedError, TypeError, and default emit only UI_STATUS_UPDATE + PERMISSION_ERROR. ui.js only listens to PERMISSION_DENIED (line 269 -> disableMicButton) and PERMISSION_ERROR is never consumed by ui.js (grep confirms no ui listener). So for 'mic in use' / 'no microphone', the error text shows but no component reacts to PERMISSION_ERROR; recovery relies on startRecordingFlow seeing stream===null and returning to IDLE (audio-handler.js:131-135), which is fine, but the categorization is inconsistent.

getPermissionStatus swallows all Permissions-API errors at debug level and returns null (the catch only logs via permLogger.debug). On a browser without the Permissions API this is intended, but a genuine query failure is invisible in production (logger defaults to ERROR level in production, logger.js:104), so the swallow is silent.

parseResponse (the public legacy helper) iterates all adapters and swallows every parse error in an empty catch, only throwing UNKNOWN_API_RESPONSE if all fail. The active transcribe() path uses adapter.parseResponse directly (line 78) so this is dead-ish, but the swallow-all pattern means a malformed-but-recognized response shape is indistinguishable from a totally unknown one.

fetch in _fetchWithRetry has no AbortController/timeout. A connection that opens but never responds (or a very large slow upload) leaves transcribe() awaiting forever; the state machine stays in PROCESSING with the mic button disabled (recording-state-machine.js:228-237) and no timeout to force the ERROR state. There is no user-facing cancel during PROCESSING either.

risks: No fetch timeout/AbortController: a hung Azure request strands the state machine in PROCESSING with the mic disabled and no recovery path short of a page reload (api-client.js:137-139, recording-state-machine.js:228-237).
RECORDING_ERROR is emitted by audio-handler.js:169 and :227 but has no consumer that resets state, so a stop()/recorder failure can leave the machine stuck in STOPPING.

Triple error surfacing (ERROR_OCCURRED + API_REQUEST_ERROR + state-machine UI_STATUS_UPDATE) means the displayed message depends on synchronous emit ordering; correctness is currently incidental, not designed.

Retry button is offered for non-retryable errors (401/403/400) because canRetry is derived from blob presence, not error retryability (audio-handler.js:320, 344) — users will retry into the same failure.
Production logger level is ERROR (logger.js:104), so warn/info/debug diagnostics for swallowed permission and parse failures are invisible in the field, making remote diagnosis hard.

bugsFound.title: No request timeout/abort leaves state machine stuck in PROCESSING on a hung request
bugsFound.description: fetch() at api-client.js:139 is called with no AbortController and no timeout. If the network stalls after the connection opens (common on flaky mobile/Wi-Fi, or a slow large upload), the await never settles. transcribe() never resolves or rejects, so audio-handler.sendToAzureAPI never reaches its catch/finally, the spinner is never hidden, and the state machine remains in PROCESSING where the mic button is disabled (recording-state-machine.js:235). There is no cancel control during PROCESSING. The only recovery is a full page reload. A bounded timeout via AbortController (rejecting with an AbortError that the existing catch already treats as a normal error) would close this hole.

bugsFound.title: recorder stop() failure emits RECORDING_ERROR that no listener consumes, stranding STOPPING state
bugsFound.location: stopRecordingFlow (166-171) and safeStopRecorder (222-230); event has no consumer (grep: RECORDING_ERROR only emitted in audio-handler.js:169,227 and recording-state-machine.js:273)
bugsFound.description: If MediaRecorder.stop() throws, safeStopRecorder/stopRecordingFlow catch it and emit APP_EVENTS.RECORDING_ERROR with the message. But the only place RECORDING_ERROR is *consumed* is implicitly via the state machine's own handleErrorState emitting it (recording-state-machine.js:273) — nothing listens to RECORDING_ERROR to drive a transition to ERROR or reset the UI. The state machine was already moved to STOPPING (audio-handler.js:159) and the 'stop' MediaRecorder event that would normally drive STOPPING->PROCESSING->IDLE will never fire because stop() failed. The machine is left in STOPPING (UI_STATUS shows 'Finishing recording', spinner logic partially run) with no automatic recovery. The mic button happens to stay clickable, but toggleRecording in STOPPING only matches canInvokeStop (audio-handler.js:79), so clicking re-enters stopRecordingFlow and re-fails.

bugsFound.title: Retry button offered for permanently non-retryable errors (401/403/400/bad config)
bugsFound.location: processAndSendAudio (306-323), retryPendingTranscription (325-346); canRetry derived at lines 320,344
bugsFound.description: pendingRetryBlob is set unconditionally when audio is captured (audio-handler.js:308) and is only cleared on success or on a fresh recording. On failure it is NOT cleared (the catch in sendToAzureAPI calls cleanup() which does not touch pendingRetryBlob). Therefore canRetry = Boolean(this.pendingRetryBlob) at line 320 is true for ALL transcription errors, including 401 (bad key), 403, and 400 (malformed request / unsupported audio) — errors that are deterministic and will fail identically on retry. The retry button (ui.js:240 toggleRetryButton(Boolean(data.canRetry))) thus invites a guaranteed-to-fail retry. The api-client already distinguishes retryable status codes (RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES, api-client.js:12) and attaches error.apiContext.status, so the information to gate the retry button correctly is available but unused.

bugsFound.title: _fetchWithRetry can fall through to return undefined if loop invariant changes
bugsFound.description: The for-loop returns the response only inside the loop body (line 142). There is no return after the loop. Currently the guard `attempt === MAX_TRANSCRIPTION_RETRIES` (line 141) forces a return on the final iteration, so undefined is unreachable today (hence 'likely', not a live bug). But it is a latent trap: any edit to the loop bound, the guard, or the constant would make the function return undefined, which flows to `if (!response.ok)` at line 70 and throws `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'ok')` — an opaque error surfaced to the user as the transcription failure message. A trailing `throw`/return after the loop would make the invariant explicit.

bugsFound.title: Decode/empty-audio failures categorized as generic errors with no specific message
bugsFound.location: convertToWav (16-25), invoked from mai-transcribe.js:22 inside transcribe try (api-client.js:54)
bugsFound.description: convertToWav awaits decodeAudioData (audio-converter.js:20). For an empty or too-short recording (e.g. user taps stop instantly) or an undecodable blob, decodeAudioData rejects with a browser EncodingError. This is caught by transcribe's outer try and surfaced via the generic error pipeline. Because navigator.onLine is true and the message does not match the network regex (audio-handler.js:379-383), the user sees a raw 'Error: <EncodingError text>. Tap mic to retry' (recording-state-machine.js:275). Retrying re-decodes the same bad blob and fails identically. There is no MESSAGES entry for an audio-decode/empty-recording failure and no length check on audioChunks before sending.

improvementOptions.title: Add an AbortController-based request timeout to _fetchWithRetry
improvementOptions.description: Wrap each fetch in api-client.js:139 with an AbortController whose timer (e.g. 60-120s, or per-attempt) aborts the request. AbortError is already a TypeError-adjacent path; map it to a clear 'Request timed out' message and let the existing catch flow it into the ERROR state so the mic re-enables. This is the single highest-value fix: it closes the only path that strands the state machine permanently. No new dependency.

improvementOptions.title: Gate the retry button on error retryability, not blob presence
improvementOptions.description: transcribe already attaches error.apiContext.status. Thread the status (or an isRetryable flag derived from RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES plus network errors) back through sendToAzureAPI's return so processAndSendAudio sets canRetry only for transient failures (429/5xx/network/timeout), not for 400/401/403. Avoids inviting guaranteed-fail retries. Small, well-bounded change at audio-handler.js:374-385 and 317-322.

improvementOptions.title: Consume RECORDING_ERROR to force the state machine into ERROR
improvementOptions.description: Either have audio-handler.js stopRecordingFlow/safeStopRecorder transition the state machine to ERROR on a stop() failure (instead of only emitting RECORDING_ERROR), or add a single listener that does so. This removes the only way to get stuck in STOPPING. Right ownership boundary: audio-handler already owns the state machine reference, so transitionTo(ERROR, {error}) in the catch at lines 169 and 227 is the minimal fix.

improvementOptions.title: Collapse the triple error-surfacing into one authoritative message path
improvementOptions.description: Today errorHandler.handleError (ERROR_OCCURRED->showError), API_REQUEST_ERROR, and the state machine's UI_STATUS_UPDATE all fire for one transcription error, with the visible result decided by emit ordering. Make errorHandler/ERROR_OCCURRED log-only (drop the ui.js:259 showError, or have it not call setStatus when the state machine will), so the state machine's ERROR message ('...Tap mic to retry') is the single source of on-screen truth. Reduces flicker and removes ordering-dependent correctness.

improvementOptions.title: Add an empty/too-short recording guard and a decode-specific message
improvementOptions.description: Before sending, check audioChunks/blob size (audio-handler.js processAndSendAudio) and short-circuit with a friendly 'Recording was empty, please try again' message; catch decodeAudioData failures in convertToWav and rethrow a typed error mapped to a MESSAGES.AUDIO_DECODE_FAILED string. Prevents the misleading network-style retry hint on undecodable audio.

improvementOptions.title: Add a defensive return/throw after the _fetchWithRetry loop
improvementOptions.description: Append an explicit `throw new Error(...)` or final return after the for-loop in api-client.js:162 so the function can never return undefined if the loop invariant is later changed. Documents intent and prevents an opaque future TypeError at line 70.

review.currentState: Runtime performance is healthy for the app's intended workload (short-to-medium dictation sessions). The hot path is the visualization, which is well-engineered: js/visualization.js pre-builds a 33-entry quantized fillStyle string table at start() (lines 92-99) and reuses a single Uint8Array for time-domain samples (line 35, read at line 72), so the per-frame draw() loop (lines 112-151) performs ZERO heap allocations — no new strings, arrays, or objects are created inside the RAF callback. The rolling amplitude buffer is hard-bounded to maxBars via push/shift (lines 106-109), so it cannot grow unbounded regardless of recording length. The audio sampler runs on a 100ms setInterval (line 104, SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS=12) decoupled from the ~60fps RAF redraw. EventBus history is explicitly capped at 50 entries with shift() on overflow (event-bus.js lines 113-116). MediaRecorder chunks are accumulated in audioChunks (audio-handler.js line 188) and cleared after processing (line 312, 413). The two genuine runtime concerns are: (1) the WAV conversion path for MAI-Transcribe does several full-buffer passes plus a per-sample DataView write loop on the main thread, which will jank/block the UI for long recordings; (2) the visualization redraws the full canvas at RAF rate even though the underlying data only changes every 100ms, so roughly 5 of every 6 frames are wasted identical redraws. Neither is a correctness bug; both are efficiency opportunities. Bundle size is explicitly out of scope (32.85kB brotli) and the no-build ES-module constraint is respected.

evidence.detail: start() pre-builds fillStyles = new Array(ALPHA_STEPS+1) of rgba() strings ONCE before the loop, and quantize(alpha) (line 99) returns a cached entry via array index. This is the correct pattern: no per-bar string allocation in the draw loop. Confirms the draw path is allocation-free.

The draw() RAF callback: re-fills the entire canvas (fillRect line 116) and iterates ALL bars (loop line 124) doing beginPath/roundRect/fill per bar every frame. I read the full loop body — no allocations, but the full redraw happens at RAF cadence (~60fps) while the data source (amplitudeHistory) only mutates every 100ms via the sampler. ~5/6 frames redraw identical pixels.
setInterval sampler at SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS (100ms) pushes one amplitude and shifts when length>maxBars. Buffer is O(maxBars) bounded; memory does NOT grow with recording duration. Confirmed bound.

fftSize=256, timeDomainData=Uint8Array(256) allocated once in constructor and reused. _sampleAmplitude does a 256-iteration RMS loop only every 100ms (called from the sampler, NOT from RAF) — negligible cost, correctly off the per-frame path.

stop() fully tears down: clearInterval, cancelAnimationFrame, removeEventListener('resize'), source.disconnect(), audioContext.close(), and amplitudeHistory=[]. No leaked timers/RAF/AudioContext per recording cycle. Clean lifecycle.

VISUALIZATION_START handler stops/nulls any prior controller before creating a new one (lines 309-312), and VISUALIZATION_STOP stops/nulls + clears (lines 329-334). Prevents accumulating multiple live AudioContexts/RAF loops across record cycles.

convertToWav runs entirely on the main thread: decodeAudioData, an OfflineAudioContext resample render, downmixToMono (full-length loop), then encodeWav with a per-sample DataView.setInt16 loop (lines 116-121). For a 5-minute recording at 16kHz mono that is ~4.8M iterations of setInt16 plus the decode/resample, all synchronous on the main thread between user stop and network send.
downmixToMono for multi-channel does channelCount * length divisions and additions; getChannelData(0) fast-path only for already-mono. Extra full-buffer pass for stereo input before the encode pass.

emit() unconditionally pushes every event into eventHistory (even in production / debugMode=false) and trims to 50 via shift(). The push+object literal {eventName,data,timestamp} happens on every emit regardless of whether history is ever read. Bounded but always-on allocation.

emit() copies the listener array ([...this.events.get(eventName)]) on every dispatch to guard against mutation during iteration. For this app's low emit frequency this is fine; noted as micro-cost only.

processAndSendAudio creates the audio Blob and stores it in pendingRetryBlob (line 308); it is held until a successful transcription clears it (line 315) or a new recording starts (startRecordingFlow line 102). One full recording's audio is retained in memory during the error/retry window — expected, bounded to one blob.

displayTranscription appends to transcriptElement.value with += '\n\n'+text and never trims. Each append creates a new full string copy of the entire transcript (textarea value is a string). Over a very long session with many transcriptions this is O(n^2) total work, but n is human-typed dictation scale — minor.

risks: WAV conversion (js/audio-converter.js) is fully synchronous on the main thread; for multi-minute MAI-Transcribe recordings the decode+resample+per-sample encode loop will freeze the UI (no spinner animation, unresponsive buttons) for hundreds of ms to seconds right after the user stops recording. This is the single most impactful real runtime cost.

No document.hidden / visibilitychange handling in visualization.js. RAF auto-throttles when the tab is hidden (so draw() largely pauses), but the 100ms setInterval sampler (line 104) keeps running and calling getByteTimeDomainData while backgrounded. Low cost because the buffer is bounded, but it is needless work while not visible.

The RAF draw loop redraws the entire canvas every frame even though amplitude data only changes every 100ms — ~5/6 frames are wasted identical full-canvas redraws. On low-end devices this is unnecessary GPU/CPU churn during recording.
eventBus.eventHistory push/trim runs on every emit even when nobody consumes getHistory() in production; pure overhead, though tiny and bounded.

bugsFound.title: Synchronous WAV encoding blocks the main thread for long MAI-Transcribe recordings
bugsFound.description: convertToWav runs decodeAudioData, an OfflineAudioContext resample render, a full-length downmix pass, and then encodeWav which iterates every sample calling DataView.setInt16 (lines 116-121). All of this executes synchronously on the main thread (called from mai-transcribe.js buildRequest line 22, which is awaited inside the transcribe flow). For a 16kHz mono recording, encodeWav alone runs sampleRate*durationSeconds iterations (~4.8M for 5 minutes). During this window the page is frozen: the visualization has already stopped, but the status spinner and any UI updates cannot paint, and the tab is unresponsive. Failure scenario: user records a long dictation with MAI-Transcribe selected, hits stop, and the UI hangs for a noticeable period before the network request even starts. This only affects the MAI-Transcribe adapter (Whisper sends WebM directly with no conversion).

bugsFound.title: Visualization redraws full canvas at RAF rate despite 100ms data cadence
bugsFound.location: draw() RAF callback (112-151) vs sampler setInterval (104-110)
bugsFound.description: The draw() callback re-clears the full canvas (fillRect line 116) and re-renders every bar (loop 124-151) on every animation frame (~60fps), but amplitudeHistory is only mutated every SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS=100ms by the sampler. Because the waveform scrolls only when a new sample is pushed, roughly 5 of every 6 frames produce a pixel-identical result. This is not a correctness bug and the loop is allocation-free, but it is wasted CPU/GPU work during the entire recording. The redraw could be gated to run only when a new sample has arrived (e.g. a dirty flag set in the sampler), cutting redraw work by ~6x with no visual change.

improvementOptions.title: Move WAV conversion off the main thread (or chunk the encode loop)
improvementOptions.description: Run convertToWav (js/audio-converter.js) inside a Web Worker, or at minimum yield the encodeWav per-sample loop in chunks via await/setTimeout so the main thread can paint the spinner. Web Worker is the clean fix: post the decoded Float32Array (transferable) and encode WAV off-thread. No new dependency needed — Workers are native. This removes the post-stop UI freeze for long MAI-Transcribe recordings, the single most impactful runtime cost found. Keep the existing synchronous path as fallback if Worker construction fails to preserve current behavior.

improvementOptions.title: Gate the visualization redraw to only run when a new sample arrives
improvementOptions.description: Add a dirty flag (e.g. this._needsRedraw) set to true in the sampler setInterval (visualization.js line 104-110) and checked at the top of draw() (line 112); skip the fillRect+bar loop when not dirty, only keeping the requestAnimationFrame re-arm. This cuts full-canvas redraws from ~60fps to ~10fps with zero visual difference (the waveform only changes every 100ms anyway), reducing CPU/GPU load during recording on low-end devices. Small, self-contained, no behavior change.

improvementOptions.title: Pause the amplitude sampler when the tab is hidden
improvementOptions.description: Add a visibilitychange listener in visualization.js that suspends the setInterval sampler (line 104) when document.hidden is true and resumes on visibility. RAF already pauses the draw loop when backgrounded, but the 100ms sampler keeps calling getByteTimeDomainData needlessly. Minor savings; bounded buffer means no correctness impact, so this is a polish item, not a blocker.

improvementOptions.title: Make eventBus history allocation conditional on debugMode
improvementOptions.description: In event-bus.js emit() (lines 107-116), only push to eventHistory when this.debugMode is true (or behind a flag), since getHistory() is a debugging affordance and the push+object-literal runs on every emit in production. Trivially removes per-emit allocation when not debugging. Very low impact given low emit frequency; include only if touching the file.

review.currentState: The app has a reasonable accessibility baseline for a no-build ES-module project: every interactive control has an aria-label or visible label, decorative SVGs are aria-hidden="true" (index.html lines 36, 44, 87, 99, 110, 119, 162, 165, 171, 180, 186), the settings modal is marked role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby="modal-title" (index.html:200-205), the sidebar is an aside with aria-label (index.html:32), and the live status line has aria-live="polite" (index.html:157). Form inputs are correctly associated via for/id (input-device, model-select, whisper-uri/key, mai-transcribe-uri/key). The pause button's aria-label is updated Pause/Resume in JS (js/ui.js:580-591) and the retry button toggles aria-hidden/disabled/label (js/ui.js:561-571). However the accessibility implementation stops at static markup: there is NO focus management anywhere except a single transcript.focus() (js/ui.js:509). The settings modal has no focus trap, no initial-focus, no focus-restore, and no Escape-to-close handler. The sidebar (a focusable region with selects/toggles) is opened on hover/click but never receives focus and is not inert when closed. CSS has exactly ONE :focus-visible rule (the noise toggle, styles.css:326); all other controls rely on :focus with outline:none on text/select fields and on a barely-visible glow ring. There is NO prefers-reduced-motion block at all despite multiple animations (cardFadeIn, modalSlideUp, spin, mic transforms). The primary live-region status text uses --text-muted, which fails WCAG AA contrast in both themes.

evidence.detail: Settings modal markup is correct (role=dialog, aria-modal, aria-labelledby=modal-title) but the close button at line 205 uses the literal × glyph as its visible content with aria-label="Close settings dialog" — fine — yet nothing in JS moves focus into the dialog on open or back to #settings-button on close.

openSettingsModal()/closeSettingsModal() only toggle settingsModal.style.display block/none and emit events. No element.focus(), no saved trigger element, no focus trap, no aria-hidden/inert toggling of the background. Keyboard focus stays on the now-hidden #settings-button behind the backdrop after open.

The ONLY keydown/Escape handler in the codebase closes the SIDEBAR (pinned/hover-preview). There is no Escape handler for the settings MODAL — confirmed by grep: the only keydown listeners in js/ are settings.js:291 (add) and 726 (remove). A keyboard user cannot dismiss the API Settings dialog with Escape; only the visible close button (×) or backdrop click works.

On validation failure saveSettings() emits UI_STATUS_UPDATE (routed to the #status element) and RETURNS WITHOUT closing the modal. The error text is written to #status (index.html:157), which lives in the controls area OUTSIDE and visually behind the modal-backdrop.
