A screen-reader/keyboard user focused inside the open dialog gets no in-dialog error; SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR has no eventBus.on consumer anywhere (grep shows only emits at settings.js:530,590, no listeners).
.sr-only utility class is fully defined but grep confirms it is NEVER used in index.html or any js/ file — there is no visually-hidden labelling/announcement infrastructure in use.

grep for prefers-reduced-motion / reduced-motion returns NONE across css, html, and js. Animations spin (0.8s infinite, styles.css:636), cardFadeIn (styles.css:447-461), modalSlideUp (styles.css:757), and mic-icon transforms run unconditionally for users who set the OS reduce-motion preference.

Text inputs, selects, and the transcript textarea set outline:none and replace it with box-shadow:var(--focus-ring). --focus-ring is 0 0 0 2px var(--accent-glow); accent-glow is rgba(accent,0.15) light / 0.20 dark — a near-invisible tint. Only ONE true :focus-visible rule exists (styles.css:326, the noise toggle). Icon buttons (.icon-btn, .header-btn, .panel-footer-btn, .mic-button, .clear-btn, .close-button, .save-button) define hover styling but NO focus/focus-visible style, so keyboard focus on the record/pause/cancel/retry/theme/settings buttons shows only the UA default ring (which the glow-ring pattern trains users away from).

Light theme: #status text-muted #9CA3AF on bg-surface #FFFFFF = 2.54:1 (FAIL, need 4.5). spinner-text and panel-sublabel same 2.54:1. input placeholder #9CA3AF on inset #F5F6FC = 2.35:1. save-button label #EEF0FB on accent #5B6EF5 = 3.70:1 (FAIL for normal text). Dark theme: #status #6B7294 on #151929 = 3.71:1 (FAIL). text-muted on bg-inset = 3.37:1 (FAIL). text-primary passes strongly in both (12.78 / 15.47).

The <canvas id=visualizer> has no aria-hidden (it conveys no text so is harmless but unlabeled). The #timer (recording elapsed time) has no live region. The #spinner-container 'Processing your audio...' text has no aria-live and is shown purely via display:block (js/ui.js:529-543), so the processing state is never announced to screen readers — only the separate #status line carries spoken state.

Heading hierarchy has two

whisper.

(sidebar line 41 + header line 125) — duplicate H1 landmarks. The document has no h1 describing the page content area; the main region (div role=main, line 133) starts at

Transcription

. role=main is applied to a div rather than a
element (acceptable but non-semantic).

risks: Keyboard-only users can tab into the open settings modal but focus is never moved there and is never trapped, so Tab walks out of the dialog into the hidden page behind the backdrop; combined with no Escape-to-close, a keyboard user can get stuck or lose context.

Screen-reader users get no spoken feedback for the 'Processing your audio...' spinner state and no in-dialog validation errors; the only live region (#status) sits behind the modal and uses the lowest-contrast text color.

The dominant status/feedback channel (#status, spinner-text, placeholders, sublabels) fails WCAG AA contrast in BOTH themes, so low-vision sighted users miss the very messages the app relies on for state communication (Recording..., paused, errors, API-not-configured).
No prefers-reduced-motion support means vestibular-sensitive users get the spinner, card fade-in, modal slide, and mic micro-animations with no opt-out.

Focus indicators on the core record/pause/cancel buttons are effectively absent (no focus-visible rule + the glow-ring pattern is near-invisible), undermining keyboard discoverability of the primary workflow.

bugsFound.title: Settings modal has no focus trap, initial focus, or focus restoration
bugsFound.description: openSettingsModal only sets settingsModal.style.display='block' and closeSettingsModal sets it to 'none'. Neither moves keyboard focus into the dialog, traps Tab within it, nor restores focus to the invoking #settings-button on close. Because role=dialog/aria-modal=true is declared (index.html:200) but not backed by focus management, AT users are told it is a modal while Tab order still includes the entire background page sitting behind the blurred backdrop. Repro: open settings via keyboard, press Tab — focus leaves the dialog into the hidden controls.

bugsFound.title: Settings modal cannot be closed with the Escape key
bugsFound.description: The single keydown handler in the app (settings.js:281) only closes the sidebar (unpinSidebar / _hideHoverPreview). grep confirms no other keydown listener exists. The settings modal (role=dialog aria-modal) therefore ignores Escape entirely; a keyboard or screen-reader user must locate and activate the × button or click the backdrop, violating the expected dialog dismissal pattern.

bugsFound.title: Validation errors are shown outside the open dialog and never announced in-context
bugsFound.description: When getValidationErrors() returns errors, saveSettings emits UI_STATUS_UPDATE (written to #status at index.html:157, in the controls card) and returns without closing the modal. The modal stays open over the page, so the error message appears behind the backdrop, outside the dialog the user is focused in. SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR is emitted but has zero eventBus.on consumers, so the structured error list is dropped; only errors[0] reaches the low-contrast #status line. A keyboard/SR user inside the dialog receives no in-dialog error feedback.

bugsFound.title: Primary live-region status text fails WCAG AA contrast in both themes
bugsFound.description: #status (the app's main feedback channel and only aria-live region) uses --text-muted: light #9CA3AF on #FFFFFF = 2.54:1, dark #6B7294 on #151929 = 3.71:1 — both below the 4.5:1 AA threshold for normal text. spinner-text and panel-sublabel share #9CA3AF (2.54:1). Input placeholders are 2.35:1. The save-button label (#EEF0FB on accent #5B6EF5) is 3.70:1 in light mode. This means the exact messages the app depends on to convey state (Recording, paused, errors) are the hardest to read for low-vision users.

bugsFound.title: No prefers-reduced-motion handling for any animation
bugsFound.description: grep for prefers-reduced-motion across css/html/js returns nothing. The continuously-spinning processing spinner, the staggered card fade-in on load (animation-delay scaled by --card-index), the modal slide-up, and mic-button micro-animations all run regardless of the user's OS 'reduce motion' setting. No @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) block disables or shortens them.

bugsFound.title: Core control buttons have no visible keyboard focus indicator
bugsFound.description: The record (#mic-button), pause, cancel, retry, theme, settings, and Cut buttons define :hover styling but no :focus or :focus-visible rule. Text fields/selects/transcript use outline:none + a near-invisible accent-glow box-shadow ring (--focus-ring = rgba accent at 0.15/0.20). The only genuine :focus-visible style in the stylesheet is the noise toggle (line 326). Keyboard focus on the primary record/pause/cancel workflow falls back to the UA default outline only, which is inconsistent with the rest of the UI; classified likely because UA defaults provide some (browser-dependent) ring, but the app supplies none of its own for these controls.

improvementOptions.title: Add focus management + Escape close to the settings modal
improvementOptions.description: In openSettingsModal() store document.activeElement, then focus the first field (e.g. settings-model-select or close-modal); add a keydown handler scoped to the modal that (a) closes on Escape and (b) traps Tab/Shift+Tab between the first and last focusable elements; in closeSettingsModal() restore focus to the stored trigger. This makes the existing role=dialog/aria-modal markup actually behave like a modal. No new dependency — ~25 lines in js/settings.js mirroring the existing _panelEscHandler pattern.

improvementOptions.title: Show validation errors inside the dialog and announce them
improvementOptions.description: Add a visually-rendered error container inside .modal-content with role=alert (or aria-live=assertive) and populate it from the SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR event (which currently has no consumer) instead of routing the error to the off-modal #status line. Keep the modal open on failure (already does) but surface the message where the user is focused. Reuses the already-emitted {errors} payload.

improvementOptions.title: Fix contrast of the status/feedback text in both themes
improvementOptions.description: Raise --text-muted (or override .status/.spinner-text/.panel-sublabel/placeholder color) so the primary aria-live channel meets 4.5:1: e.g. light #6B7280 already passes at 4.83:1 vs white, dark needs ~#8B92B8+. Also darken/lighten the save-button label or button bg to clear 4.5:1. Token-only change in css/styles.css :root and .dark-theme.

improvementOptions.title: Add a prefers-reduced-motion media block
improvementOptions.description: Add @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce){ *,*::before,*::after { animation-duration:0.01ms !important; animation-iteration-count:1 !important; transition-duration:0.01ms !important; } } plus a non-spinning spinner fallback. Single CSS block; respects OS setting without touching JS.

improvementOptions.title: Provide explicit :focus-visible styles for all interactive controls
improvementOptions.description: Add a shared :focus-visible rule giving .mic-button, .icon-btn, .header-btn, .panel-footer-btn, .clear-btn, .close-button, .save-button a clearly visible outline (e.g. 2px solid var(--accent) with offset), and strengthen --focus-ring to a solid accent ring rather than the 0.15-opacity glow. Improves keyboard operability of the entire record/pause/cancel/settings workflow. CSS-only.

improvementOptions.title: Announce processing state and make the canvas/timer SR-friendly
improvementOptions.description: Add aria-live=polite (or aria-busy on the controls region) so the 'Processing your audio...' state is spoken; the spinner is currently display-toggled with no announcement (js/ui.js:529-543). Optionally add aria-hidden=true to <canvas id=visualizer> (purely decorative) and consider role=timer/aria-live on #timer if elapsed time should be conveyed. Small HTML + minor JS.

improvementOptions.title: Make the closed sidebar inert and manage its focus on open
improvementOptions.description: When the sidebar is hidden (transform translateX(-100%)) its selects/toggles remain in the Tab order. Add inert (or aria-hidden + tabindex management) when closed, and move focus into the panel when pinned/hover-opened via keyboard, restoring it to #panel-toggle on close — mirroring the modal fix. Prevents keyboard users tabbing into an off-screen panel.

improvementOptions.title: Resolve duplicate H1 and use semantic landmarks
improvementOptions.description:
Two

whisper.

exist (index.html:41 sidebar, :125 header); make one an aria-hidden decorative wordmark or downgrade, and consider replacing
with a real
element. Minor markup cleanup for cleaner landmark/heading navigation.

review.currentState:
The suite is substantial and green: 25 test files, 305 tests, all passing in ~6.6s (verified via `npx vitest run`). Coverage thresholds are enforced in vitest.config.js (statements 85, branches 80, functions 70, lines 85) with `all: true`, so coverage is genuinely measured against all js/** files except main.js. The strongest layer is the pure/logic modules: recording-state-machine.js (21/21 fns, 376/376 stmts), event-bus.js (9/9), audio-converter.js (5/5), and the model-adapters (every adapter file fully covered). tests/recording-state-machine.vitest.js and tests/model-adapters.vitest.js are genuinely behavioral — they assert real transition rules from the STATE_TRANSITIONS constant, real FormData field construction per model, real header selection, and real parseResponse shape-sniffing, which is exactly the kind of high-value contract testing you want. The api-client-errors and model-adapters tests use realistic fetch mocks (status, headers.get, json/text) and assert observable outcomes (thrown messages, emitted events, retry counts of 6 for 429/500/503).

However, test quality degrades sharply at the integration and UI boundaries. The big "integration" files (recording-integration, audio-handler-integration, ui-event-bus-proper) are mostly event-emission spying and shallow state assertions rather than true end-to-end exercises, and several contain mislabeled or tautological cases. ui.js sits at 16/24 functions (66%) — the entire app-wiring/theme/prerequisite surface (init, loadTheme, applyTheme, checkBrowserSupport, checkRecordingPrerequisites's full branch set, enableMicrophoneAfterFix, showError, stopVisualization) is largely unexercised by direct UI tests; what coverage exists for checkRecordingPrerequisites comes indirectly and, in the workflow file, is actively stubbed out. settings.js is 26/31 fns / 568/677 stmts, with the sidebar/device-enumeration methods (populateDeviceList, _populateDeviceListIfStale, unpinSidebar, _isSidebarPinned, _panelEscHandler) untested.

evidence.detail: The two 'Complete workflow integration' tests replace ui.checkRecordingPrerequisites with a vi.fn().mockImplementation that RE-IMPLEMENTS the production branch inline (reads config, calls ui.enableMicButton + ui.setStatus), then asserts enableMicButton/setStatus were called and checkRecordingPrerequisites 'was called'. The test verifies its own mock, not js/ui.js production logic. The real checkRecordingPrerequisites (ui.js:431-453) is never executed in these cases — a textbook tautological test.

Test 'cleans up resources after MediaRecorder errors' calls mediaRecorderEventHandlers.error.forEach(handler => handler({error})). But js/audio-handler.js startRecording (lines 187-208) only registers 'dataavailable' and 'stop' listeners — there is NO 'error' listener (grep confirms no addEventListener('error') / onerror in audio-handler.js). So error.forEach iterates an empty array and runs zero production error-handling code; cleanup is actually driven by the later normal stopRecordingFlow. The test name asserts a behavior that does not exist.

All eight named spies (recordingStartedSpy, recordingStoppedSpy, stateChangeSpy, transcriptionReadySpy, etc.) are literally aliases to the SAME eventBusEmitSpy = vi.spyOn(eventBus,'emit'). The descriptive names imply granular verification but every assertion just checks eventBus.emit was called with some args. This is emission-spying, not behavioral verification of distinct outcomes.

destroy() test asserts audioHandler._unsubscribers.length === 5 — a magic number hard-coupled to the exact count of eventBus.on calls in setupEventBusListeners (audio-handler.js:51-57). Adding any new listener (very likely in 2.0) breaks this test even though destroy() still works correctly. Change-detector coupling to an implementation count.

Tests assert CSS/HTML ABSENCE via regex against raw file text: html not contains 'mic-glow'/'mic-ring', css not contains 'glowPulse'/'ringExpand'/'@keyframes recordPulse', and getCssBlock('.mic-button') not matching /transition: all/. getCssBlock uses `selector\s*\{[^}]*\}` and takes the FIRST match — order-dependent against css/styles.css which has three '.mic-button' blocks (lines 571, 938, 987). These guard against regressed past bugs (commits 4c63924/c3a2202) but are brittle: any benign CSS refactor (renaming a class, reordering media-query blocks, re-adding a non-expanding animation) can flip them with no behavioral meaning.

API Failure Recovery tests do `global.MediaRecorder = vi.fn(() => mockMediaRecorder)` permanently mid-suite, and rely on real setTimeout(...,50) waits while other blocks use vi.useFakeTimers(). The MockMediaRecorder defined at top (lines 66-166) is bypassed. This mixed fake/real-timer + hand-rolled-recorder approach makes these 'integration' tests fragile and inconsistent with the suite's own infrastructure.

RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED handler tests spy on UI methods (vi.spyOn(ui,'enableMicButton') etc.) and assert the spy was called, but the spies do not run real DOM logic meaningfully and the real micButton.classList branch in ui.js:227 ('stopping' state) is not asserted. These verify the switch dispatch wiring, which is fine, but they are method-call assertions, not DOM-state assertions — they would not catch a regression where enableMicButton sets the wrong opacity/cursor.

Coverage parse of coverage/coverage-final.json shows ui.js uncovered functions: init, loadTheme, applyTheme, checkBrowserSupport, enableMicrophoneAfterFix, showError, openSettingsModal, stopVisualization (16/24 fns). The theme system (applyTheme reads localStorage + matchMedia + toggles dark-theme + paints canvas) and full app bootstrapping (init) have zero direct unit tests despite being core user-visible behavior.

Test runs execSync('npx jsdoc --configure jsdoc.json') with stdio ignore. This is a build/tooling smoke test masquerading as a unit test: it shells out, depends on cwd and the jsdoc binary, contributes nothing to behavioral coverage, and adds real-process latency/flakiness risk to the suite.

Positive evidence: the 'Transcription retry' suite IS genuinely behavioral — it drives processAndSendAudio with a rejecting-then-resolving transcribe mock, asserts pendingRetryBlob is a real Blob, that retryPendingTranscription re-sends the SAME blob instance (mock.calls[1][0] === failedAudioBlob), and that state ends IDLE. This exercises the real audio-handler.js retry path (lines 306-346) end-to-end and is the model the integration tests should follow.

risks: A meaningful slice of the 'integration' suite is emission-spying and method-call-spying rather than end-to-end behavior, so reported coverage overstates confidence: state-machine + adapters are well tested, but the UI rendering layer and app bootstrap are thin. A regression in applyTheme, init wiring, or checkRecordingPrerequisites's actual DOM effects could pass CI.

Tautological tests (settings-workflow lines 236-243/284-291) give false assurance — they will stay green even if ui.checkRecordingPrerequisites is broken, because the production method is stubbed and replaced by an inline copy.

Mislabeled tests (audio-handler-integration 'MediaRecorder errors') mean the MediaRecorder error path is effectively UNTESTED while appearing tested; if 2.0 adds a real 'error' listener, no existing test validates it.

Change-detector coupling (_unsubscribers.length === 5; first-match CSS regex in mic-button-effects) will produce spurious failures during normal 2.0 refactors, training the team to edit tests reflexively rather than trust them.
Mixed fake/real timers and permanent global.MediaRecorder reassignment within recording-integration risk order-dependent flakiness as more cases are added; currently green but fragile.
execSync-based jsdoc test couples the unit suite to an external binary and cwd, a CI portability/flake risk.

bugsFound.title: Tautological workflow tests verify their own mock instead of production checkRecordingPrerequisites
bugsFound.location: lines 236-243 and 284-291 ('Complete workflow integration with fixes')
bugsFound.description: Both tests call vi.spyOn(ui,'checkRecordingPrerequisites').mockImplementation(() => { const config = settings.getModelConfig(); if (config.apiKey && config.uri) { ui.enableMicButton(); ui.setStatus(DEFAULT_RESET_STATUS); } return true; }) and then assert checkRecordingPrerequisitesSpy was called, enableMicButton was called, and setStatus(DEFAULT_RESET_STATUS) was called. Because the production method is replaced, the real js/ui.js:431-453 logic (browser-support check, APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED/APP_READY emissions, config-missing path) never runs. The test passes by construction — it cannot fail if checkRecordingPrerequisites regresses. It provides false coverage signal for the most important 'settings saved -> mic enabled' user workflow.

bugsFound.title: MediaRecorder 'error' cleanup test exercises a code path that does not exist
bugsFound.location: lines 415-449 ('cleans up resources after MediaRecorder errors')
bugsFound.description: The test fires mediaRecorderEventHandlers.error.forEach(handler => handler({error})). js/audio-handler.js startRecording (187-208) registers only 'dataavailable' and 'stop' listeners; grep confirms no 'error'/onerror handler anywhere in audio-handler.js. So mediaRecorderEventHandlers.error is always empty and the forEach is a no-op. The cleanup the test then asserts is produced solely by the subsequent normal stopRecordingFlow + stop event, not by any error handling. The test's name and intent (verify resource cleanup on recorder error) are not actually validated, leaving the real-browser MediaRecorder onerror scenario untested.

bugsFound.title: destroy() test hard-codes listener count (_unsubscribers.length === 5)
bugsFound.description: expect(audioHandler._unsubscribers.length).toBe(5) couples the test to the exact number of eventBus.on subscriptions in audio-handler.js setupEventBusListeners (currently 5: API_CONFIG_MISSING, MIC/PAUSE/CANCEL/RETRY button clicks). Any 2.0 feature that adds a sixth listener breaks this assertion despite destroy() being correct. The meaningful assertions (unsubscribers emptied, listener actually removed from eventBus.events) are already present on lines 550-552; the magic-number check adds only brittleness.

bugsFound.title: mic-button-effects CSS-absence tests are order-dependent first-match string assertions
bugsFound.description: getCssBlock builds RegExp(`${selector}\s*\{[^}]*\}`,'s') and returns match[0] — the FIRST matching block. css/styles.css contains three '.mic-button' rule blocks (lines 571, 938, 987 per grep). The assertions (.mic-button block not matching /transition: all/, .mic-button.recording not containing 'animation', css not containing '@keyframes recordPulse'/'glowPulse'/'ringExpand') guard against specific reverted regressions but are durable only as long as block ordering and naming never change. A benign CSS reorganization (e.g., moving a media-query .mic-button block above line 571, or re-introducing a non-expanding keyframe with a different name) flips the result with zero behavioral meaning. These are change-detectors, not behavior tests.

improvementOptions.title: Replace the two tautological settings-workflow tests with real-method assertions
improvementOptions.description: In tests/settings-workflow.vitest.js (236-243, 284-291) stop mocking ui.checkRecordingPrerequisites. Let the real method run against the stateful mock DOM (createStatefulMockElement already supports it) and assert observable effects: micButton.disabled === false and the APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED/APP_READY emissions. This converts a guaranteed-green test into one that actually guards the save->mic-enabled workflow.

improvementOptions.title: Fix or remove the mislabeled MediaRecorder-error cleanup test
improvementOptions.description: Either (a) add a real 'error' listener to js/audio-handler.js and have the test fire it to exercise genuine error cleanup, or (b) rename/rewrite the test to reflect that cleanup happens via stopRecordingFlow. Currently it asserts a behavior that no production code provides. Decide the 2.0 intent for MediaRecorder.onerror and make the test match reality.

improvementOptions.title: Add direct UI unit tests for theme + prerequisites + bootstrap
improvementOptions.description: ui.js is at 66% functions. Add focused tests for applyTheme (localStorage='dark'/'light'/'auto' + matchMedia drives dark-theme toggle + icon display), checkRecordingPrerequisites (real branches: no browser support, missing apiKey/uri, all-good -> enableMicButton+APP_READY), and showError. These are pure-ish given the existing happy-dom + matchMedia mocks and would close the most user-visible untested surface without any new dependency.

improvementOptions.title: De-alias the recording-integration spies and assert distinct outcomes
improvementOptions.description: In recording-integration.vitest.js the eight named spies are all the same eventBus.emit spy. Either collapse them to one honestly-named spy, or assert on distinct observable end-states (final state machine state, transcript text delivered, tracks stopped) rather than just 'emit was called'. This raises the integration tests from emission-spying toward true end-to-end verification — the audio-handler-integration 'Transcription retry' suite (286-318) is the right template.

improvementOptions.title: Replace the _unsubscribers.length magic number with intent-based assertions
improvementOptions.description: Drop expect(_unsubscribers.length).toBe(5); keep/strengthen the assertions that after destroy() each subscribed event has no listeners (e.g. check eventBus.events for MIC_BUTTON_CLICKED, PAUSE/CANCEL/RETRY, API_CONFIG_MISSING all absent). Removes refactor brittleness while testing the real contract.

improvementOptions.title: Make CSS-regression tests robust or move them to a lint rule
improvementOptions.description: The mic-button-effects guards protect real past fixes but via fragile first-match regex. Either parse all '.mic-button*' blocks and assert across them, or express these invariants as a stylelint rule (no 'transition: all' on interactive controls, forbid named keyframes). A stylelint rule is more durable than string-absence tests, though it adds a dev dependency — weigh against the simplicity constraint.

improvementOptions.title: Move jsdoc-generation smoke test out of the unit suite
improvementOptions.description: tests/tooling/jsdoc-generation.vitest.js shells out via execSync('npx jsdoc ...'). Run it as a separate CI step (it already has an npm 'docs' script) rather than inside vitest, so the unit suite stays fast, hermetic, and free of cwd/binary dependence.

review.currentState: The codebase is 14 ES modules (~4,333 lines) plus a model-adapters/ subfolder, with a genuinely clean, acyclic dependency graph. Leaf modules (constants.js, logger.js, event-bus.js, error-handler.js, status-helper.js) depend downward only; main.js (23 lines) is a disciplined composition root that instantiates Settings, UI, AzureAPIClient, AudioHandler and wires nothing else — all runtime coupling flows through the singleton eventBus. The only direct class-to-class imports are main.js importing Settings and UI; everything else imports the eventBus, constants, logger, or PermissionManager. Circular-import risk is actively managed: ui.js dynamically imports visualization.js (ui.js:316) specifically to avoid a cycle. The event bus (event-bus.js, 307 lines) is a competent pub/sub with priority, once, history (capped at 50), and per-listener try/catch. APP_EVENTS centralizes 47 event-name constants under a namespace:action convention. So the decoupling mechanism is real and the macro-architecture is sound. The weaknesses are concentrated in two areas: (1) settings.js (734 lines) is the actual god module — it conflates API config/validation/model-selection with an entirely separate Notion-style sidebar feature (hover timers, pin/unpin, device enumeration, Escape handling, slide-out animation: lines 209-405), two responsibilities that share nothing; and (2) event-name sprawl plus parallel pathways — 16 of 47 events are emitted but never subscribed, ui.js subscribes to 24 events in one 190-line method, and there are two competing error channels (RECORDING_ERROR vs ERROR_OCCURRED). ui.js (654 lines), despite the prompt flagging it, is more cohesive than settings.js: it is genuinely the DOM-render layer for the event bus, though it leaks two non-UI concerns (prerequisite/config checking and a delegating settings shim).

evidence.detail: Composition root: instantiates Settings, UI, AzureAPIClient, AudioHandler, then calls ui.init(settings). No other wiring — all coupling is via eventBus. AudioHandler kept alive only by a deliberately-unused local (line 18 comment 'Reference kept to prevent GC'), an unusual lifecycle that relies on event-bus listeners holding the instance.

setupPanelListeners, _clearHoverTimers, _isSidebarPinned, _cancelHoverSlideOut, _showHoverPreview, _populateDeviceListIfStale, _hideHoverPreview, pinSidebar, unpinSidebar, populateDeviceList — ~200 lines implementing a sidebar/drawer UI feature (hover-preview state machine, transitionend animation, device enumeration) that is unrelated to the class's stated job of API configuration. Strongest split candidate in the repo: a SidePanel class.

setupEventBusListeners is a single 190-line method registering 24 separate eventBus.on subscriptions. The RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED handler (200-246) embeds an 8-case switch mapping state names (as raw string literals 'idle','recording', not RECORDING_STATES constants) to UI side effects — business-state knowledge living in the view layer.

checkRecordingPrerequisites (ui.js) and startRecordingFlow (audio-handler.js) both independently run the same gate: PermissionManager.checkBrowserSupport() then `if (!config.apiKey || !config.uri)` then emit APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED. Same logic duplicated across the view and the controller; the apiKey/uri check also recurs a third time in settings.js:695.

setStatus() in ui.js manually clears statusElement._statusTimeout — the exact private property and timeout-management that status-helper.js's showTemporaryStatus owns. Two modules read/write the same monkey-patched DOM property (_statusTimeout) as a coordination channel; brittle hidden coupling rather than a clean API.

16 events are emitted but have zero subscribers: API_REQUEST_START, API_REQUEST_SUCCESS, APP_INITIALIZED, APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED, APP_READY, DEVICE_CHANGED, PERMISSION_ERROR, PERMISSION_STATUS_CHANGED, RECORDING_CANCELLED, RECORDING_ERROR, RECORDING_PAUSED, RECORDING_RESUMED, RECORDING_STOPPED, SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR, UI_SETTINGS_OPENED, UI_SETTINGS_CLOSED. APP_ERROR is defined in APP_EVENTS (event-bus.js) but never emitted or subscribed anywhere. This is event-name sprawl: ~36% of emitted events are write-only.

Two parallel error pathways. errorHandler.handleError emits ERROR_OCCURRED (ui.js:259 → showError). Separately, the state machine's handleErrorState emits RECORDING_ERROR (no UI subscriber) AND its own UI_STATUS_UPDATE with a formatted message. audio-handler.startRecordingFlow (143-145) calls BOTH errorHandler.handleError (→ERROR_OCCURRED→showError) and transitionTo(ERROR) (→another UI_STATUS_UPDATE), so a single failure can drive the status element twice from two subsystems.

Config split-brain: getModelConfig() resolves model via getCurrentModel() = this.modelSelect.value (main toolbar selector), but updateSettingsVisibility() and all validation resolve via getCurrentModelFromSettings() = this.settingsModelSelect.value (modal selector). The two selectors are sync'd by change-listeners (120-159), but the canonical source of truth for 'which model is active' is ambiguous and split across two DOM elements.

RecordingStateMachine holds a back-reference `this.audioHandler = audioHandler` (constructed as `new RecordingStateMachine(this)` in audio-handler.js:45) but never reads it — the FSM communicates purely via eventBus. The bidirectional reference is dead coupling that misleadingly implies the FSM drives the handler directly.

MESSAGES centralizes user-facing strings, yet ui.js:555 ('Recording... Click again to stop'), ui.js:585 ('Recording paused'), ui.js:584/589 ('Resume'/'Pause'), and recording-state-machine.js:165 ('Recording... Click to stop')/185 ('Recording paused') hardcode status strings that bypass MESSAGES — and the two 'Recording...' variants differ ('Click again to stop' vs 'Click to stop') for the same logical state, set from two different modules.

risks: settings.js conflates API-config and a sidebar UI feature; any change to one risks the other and the 734-line file is the hardest to reason about in the repo.

Event-name sprawl (16 write-only events, 1 fully-dead APP_ERROR) erodes the value of APP_EVENTS as documentation — a maintainer cannot trust that an emitted event does anything, and grep gives false confidence.

Two error pathways (ERROR_OCCURRED via error-handler, RECORDING_ERROR + direct UI_STATUS_UPDATE via state machine) can both fire for one failure, causing duplicate/competing status writes and making 'where does this error message come from' hard to trace.
Hidden coupling via the monkey-patched statusElement._statusTimeout property shared between ui.js and status-helper.js: refactoring either independently can silently break temporary-status reset.
Duplicated prerequisite/config-gate logic in 3 places (ui.js, audio-handler.js, settings.js) means a future config rule change must be made consistently in three spots or behavior diverges.

Split-brain config source (modelSelect vs settingsModelSelect) relies on change-listeners staying in sync; a code path that sets one without the other would make getModelConfig disagree with validation/visibility.

bugsFound.title: Config source split-brain: getModelConfig reads a different selector than validation/visibility
bugsFound.description: getModelConfig() determines the active model from this.modelSelect.value (main toolbar), while validation (getValidationErrors:550), visibility (updateSettingsVisibility:414) and save (saveSettings:586) determine it from this.settingsModelSelect.value (modal). They are kept consistent only by the two change-listeners at lines 120-159 that copy each selector's value into the other. If any future code path mutates one selector programmatically without dispatching 'change' (e.g. setting .value directly, as loadSavedModel:102-105 and loadSettingsToForm:464-465 already do), the two selectors desync and getModelConfig() will report a different model than the one being validated/saved — e.g. credentials saved for MAI while the recorder pulls the Whisper URI/key. Not proven to fire in the current flows (the direct-set sites happen at init/open before user interaction), hence 'likely' rather than 'confirmed', but it is a fragile single-source-of-truth violation.

bugsFound.title: Single failure drives the status element twice via two independent error channels
bugsFound.location: startRecordingFlow catch block (141-148) + recording-state-machine.handleErrorState (271-280) + error-handler ERROR_OCCURRED listener (ui.js:259)
bugsFound.description: In startRecordingFlow's catch, errorHandler.handleError(err) emits ERROR_OCCURRED, which ui.js:259 turns into showError()→setStatus(message). Immediately after, transitionTo(RECORDING_STATES.ERROR,{error}) runs handleErrorState which emits its own UI_STATUS_UPDATE with a different formatted string ('Error: <msg>. Tap mic to retry'). Both write to the same status element for one underlying error, and the second overwrites the first, so the ERROR_OCCURRED message is effectively wasted work and the two error subsystems are redundant. Low severity (cosmetic/ordering, last-writer-wins) but a clear sign the two error pathways are not cleanly owned.

improvementOptions.title: Extract the sidebar/drawer feature out of settings.js into a SidePanel module
improvementOptions.description: Move setupPanelListeners, the hover-timer logic (_clearHoverTimers/_showHoverPreview/_hideHoverPreview/_cancelHoverSlideOut/_populateDeviceListIfStale), pinSidebar/unpinSidebar, populateDeviceList, the Escape handler, and the associated DOM refs (sidePanel/panelToggle/panelClose/panelBackdrop/noiseToggle/inputDeviceSelect) from settings.js (lines ~55-61, 209-405) into a new js/side-panel.js class instantiated from main.js. This cuts settings.js from 734 to ~450 lines and gives it a single responsibility (API config + model selection + validation). The two concerns share no state except localStorage keys, so the split is low-risk. Keep the no-build raw-ES-module constraint — it is just one more file in main.js's composition root.

improvementOptions.title: Collapse the duplicated prerequisite/config gate into one helper
improvementOptions.description: The triad PermissionManager.checkBrowserSupport() + (!apiKey||!uri) check appears in ui.js:431-453, audio-handler.js:108-124, and partially settings.js:695. Extract a single pure function (e.g. in a small prerequisites.js or as a static on Settings) returning {ready, reason} that both ui.js and audio-handler.js call, so a future change to config rules is made once. This removes a class of 'fixed in one place, not the other' bugs.

improvementOptions.title: Prune dead/write-only events and unify the two error channels
improvementOptions.description: Remove APP_ERROR (defined, never used) and audit the 16 emit-only events: either add the intended subscriber or delete the emit. Then pick ONE error channel — route the state machine's error display through errorHandler/ERROR_OCCURRED (or vice-versa) so a single failure produces one status write. This shrinks APP_EVENTS from 47 noisy constants toward the ~31 that actually have both a producer and consumer, restoring its value as a contract. Pure cleanup, no behavior change for the surviving paths.

improvementOptions.title: Make ui.js setStatus reuse status-helper instead of hand-rolling timeout cleanup
improvementOptions.description: ui.js setStatus (474-482) duplicates status-helper.js's _statusTimeout management on the same monkey-patched DOM property. Have setStatus call showTemporaryStatus(element, message, 'info', 0) (duration 0 = no auto-reset, already supported at status-helper.js:37) so the timeout-clearing logic lives in exactly one place and the hidden shared-property coupling is owned by one module.

improvementOptions.title: Replace raw state-name string literals in ui.js with RECORDING_STATES constants and centralize the status strings
improvementOptions.description: ui.js:206-245 switches on 'idle'/'recording'/etc. as bare strings instead of importing RECORDING_STATES (it already imports from constants.js). Same file hardcodes 'Recording... Click again to stop' (555) and 'Recording paused' (585) which duplicate, and conflict with, recording-state-machine.js:165/185 ('Recording... Click to stop'). Move these to MESSAGES and reference the constants so the FSM and the view cannot drift on the same logical state.

review.currentState: The repo ships a single 94-line README.md, a package.json pinned at version 1.0.0, and a tooling-heavy dev setup (Vitest, ESLint flat config, knip, jsdoc, size-limit, husky). There is NO LICENSE, NO CHANGELOG, NO CONTRIBUTING, NO CODE_OF_CONDUCT, NO SECURITY.md, and NO .github/ directory of any kind (confirmed: `git ls-files` shows only .env.example, .gitignore, .husky, README.md, css, eslint.config.js, index.html, js, jsdoc.json, knip.json, package-lock.json, package.json, plan, spec, tests, vitest.config.js). There are zero git tags, so 1.0.0 was never actually tagged/released. "CI" is purely local husky hooks: .husky/pre-commit runs `npm run lint`; .husky/pre-push runs `npm run test:coverage && npm run deps:check:prod`. No hosted CI (no GitHub Actions / Azure Pipelines / GitLab / CircleCI workflow files are tracked — the only yml match is a test fixture `tests/settings-workflow.vitest.js`). Generated jsdoc docs/ (40+ HTML files, regenerated 2026-06-03) and coverage/ are correctly gitignored (.gitignore lines 17-18) and NOT committed (`git ls-files docs` returns 0), so they are not stale-prone in the repo. spec/ has two thorough, current design specs (api-client v1.1 last_updated 2026-04-06; recording-state-machine v1.1 same date) and is tracked. plan/ + plan/archive (40+ files) are tracked historical artifacts. The app is a security-sensitive API-key-in-browser model: index.html (lines 1-25) has NO Content-Security-Policy meta tag, only charset + viewport; it loads Google Fonts from a third-party origin and runs an inline bootstrap <script> (lines 7-20). README's Deployment section (lines 84-90) only covers GitHub Pages with no CSP/security/threat-model discussion. The Privacy Note (lines 91-93) is the sole security statement.

evidence.detail: Deployment section is 3 GitHub Pages steps; Privacy Note is 1 sentence. No CSP, no key-storage-in-localStorage warning, no threat model, no troubleshooting section, no Azure resource creation steps (no mention of Azure portal, resource group, region, Speech resource creation, or how to obtain Target URI/API Key).

Setup/Configuration tells users to enter a 'Target URI' and 'API Key' but never explains how to create the Azure Speech resource or where in the portal these values live. Does not document the three real models (whisper, whisper-translate, mai-transcribe) — README only lists 'Azure Whisper (stable) and MAI-Transcribe (preview)' (line 9). grep for 'whisper-translate' in README = 0 hits, yet js/model-adapters/whisper-translate.js exists and spec REQ-002 lists it as a supported model.

INACCURATE: 'Pre-push hooks automatically run: Linting checks (npm run lint), Coverage tests, Production dependency validation.' Actual .husky/pre-push = `npm run test:coverage && npm run deps:check:prod` (no lint). Lint runs in .husky/pre-commit only. README conflates the two hooks.

version is "1.0.0" and "private": true. No "license" field, no "repository" field, no "author", no "homepage". `git tag` returns empty — 1.0.0 was never tagged, so there is no release history to base a 2.0 bump on.

docs/ and coverage/ are gitignored, so jsdoc HTML is NOT committed (git ls-files docs = 0). Good: avoids stale generated docs in VCS. But it also means no published API docs exist anywhere unless a deploy step runs `npm run docs` — and no such step is defined.

No <meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy">. Loads fonts.googleapis.com / fonts.gstatic.com cross-origin and an inline <script>. For an app that holds an Azure API key in localStorage, the absence of any CSP is a notable shipping gap for 2.0.

Honestly documents that credentials come from the Settings UI and live in localStorage, and that env vars are NOT consumed at runtime (lines 4-7, 17-20). This is good but it is the ONLY place the localStorage key-storage security tradeoff is written down — it belongs in README/SECURITY.md where users will see it.

Spec documents three models and exact per-model FormData/auth-header/filename differences (whisper=file+language+api-key; whisper-translate=file+api-key, no language; mai-transcribe=audio WAV+definition+Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key). README exposes none of this model-difference detail to users or contributors.
Coverage thresholds (85/80/70/85) match README lines 56-60 exactly — this part of the docs is accurate and current.

Code enforces whisper keys = 32-char hex and URI must be HTTPS, with model-specific key rules. None of these credential-format requirements are documented in README troubleshooting, so a user pasting a valid-looking Speech key against the wrong model gets an opaque 'Invalid API key format' with no doc to consult.

risks: No LICENSE file: the project is legally 'all rights reserved' by default, which blocks any external contributor or fork from legally using/modifying it — a hard blocker for an open 2.0 release.

No version policy or CHANGELOG and zero git tags: nothing defines what differentiates 1.0.0 from a 2.0 tag, so 'cut a 2.0' has no objective gate. The version in package.json is essentially decorative.
Documentation drift already present: README claims pre-push runs lint when it does not (README.md:64-71 vs .husky/pre-push). Drift this early erodes trust in the docs.

No hosted CI: all quality gates (lint, coverage, deps) live only in local husky hooks, which contributors can bypass with --no-verify and which never run on PRs from forks. A 2.0 with external contributors has no enforced gate.

Security story for API-key-in-browser is under-documented and partially unimplemented: localStorage key storage is only mentioned in .env.example; index.html ships no CSP; README Deployment omits any security guidance for a GitHub-Pages-hosted app that holds a live Azure key.
Onboarding gap: README never explains how to create the Azure Speech resource or find the Target URI / API Key, and omits whisper-translate entirely, so a brand-new 2.0 user cannot self-serve setup.

bugsFound.title: README misdescribes which git hook runs lint
bugsFound.description: README states pre-push hooks run 'Linting checks (npm run lint)' alongside coverage and dependency validation. The actual .husky/pre-push contains only `npm run test:coverage && npm run deps:check:prod` — no lint. Lint runs in .husky/pre-commit (`npm run lint`). A contributor relying on the README would believe lint is gated at push time when it is gated at commit time, and could push lint-failing code if they bypassed the commit hook. Documentation/behavior mismatch, not a runtime crash.

bugsFound.title: README undercounts/mislabels supported transcription models
bugsFound.location: README.md:9 (Features) and 31-37 (Configuration) vs js/model-adapters/ and spec REQ-002
bugsFound.description: README lists only 'Azure Whisper (stable) and MAI-Transcribe (preview)' and the model picker docs mention 'Azure Whisper or MAI-Transcribe'. The codebase ships js/model-adapters/whisper-translate.js and spec-design-api-client.md REQ-002 lists three supported models (whisper, whisper-translate, mai-transcribe). The whisper-translate model is undocumented for users, so a user selecting it has no guidance on its behavior (translation, no language param).

bugsFound.title: package.json declares no license while repo has no LICENSE file
bugsFound.location: package.json:1-51 (no license field) + repo root (no LICENSE)
bugsFound.description: package.json has no `"license"` field and the repository contains no LICENSE/LICENSE.md (ls confirmed absent). The package is `"private": true`, so npm tooling will not warn, but for a 2.0 public release this means the code is under default exclusive copyright. Any contributor, forker, or GitHub Pages re-host has no granted rights. This is a release blocker, not a code-execution bug.

improvementOptions.title: Add a LICENSE file and package.json license field
improvementOptions.description: Pick a license (MIT is consistent with a small no-build OSS tool) and add LICENSE at repo root plus `"license": "MIT"` to package.json. Without this, a 2.0 public release grants users no legal rights. Lowest-effort highest-leverage release-readiness fix.

improvementOptions.title: Define a version/release policy and tag 1.x before 2.0
improvementOptions.description: Add a short 'Versioning' section (e.g. in CONTRIBUTING or README) stating what semver bumps mean for this app, create the missing v1.0.0 tag retroactively (zero tags exist today), and define concrete 2.0 gates (e.g. 'CSP shipped + LICENSE + CHANGELOG + model docs complete'). Currently nothing distinguishes 1.0.0 from 2.0.

improvementOptions.title: Add a CHANGELOG.md
improvementOptions.description: Recent commits (retry flow, mic pulse fixes, API-key validation) are real user-facing behavior changes with no changelog. Start a Keep-a-Changelog-style file so a 2.0 entry has a home and 1.x history is captured. Pairs with the version policy.

improvementOptions.title: Expand README setup with Azure Speech resource creation + credential location steps
improvementOptions.description: Add a step-by-step: create an Azure Speech (or AI Services) resource in the portal, pick a region, copy Key + Endpoint, and map them to the app's 'API Key' and 'Target URI' fields. Document the three models (whisper, whisper-translate, mai-transcribe) and their differences using the table already in spec/spec-design-api-client.md:131-137. Today a new user cannot self-serve setup.

improvementOptions.title: Add a troubleshooting section tied to the actual validation rules
improvementOptions.description: settings.js:555-567 enforces 'whisper key = 32-char hex', 'URI must use HTTPS', and model-specific key formats, surfacing opaque messages like 'Invalid API key format'. Add a README troubleshooting table mapping each error message to its cause/fix (wrong model selected for a Speech key, http:// URI, CORS failures). Concrete and code-grounded.

improvementOptions.title: Document and harden the API-key-in-browser security model (CSP + SECURITY.md)
improvementOptions.description: index.html (lines 1-25) ships no Content-Security-Policy. Add a CSP meta tag (restrict to self + fonts.googleapis/gstatic + the configured Azure endpoint host) and write a SECURITY.md / README security section stating that the Azure key is stored in localStorage in the browser, the implications of hosting on public GitHub Pages, and recommended scoping/rotation of the key. The honest note in .env.example:4-7 should be promoted to user-facing docs.

improvementOptions.title: Fix the pre-push hook description in README
improvementOptions.description: Correct README.md:64-71 to state lint runs at pre-commit and coverage+deps run at pre-push, matching .husky/pre-commit and .husky/pre-push. One-line accuracy fix that prevents doc drift undermining contributor trust.

improvementOptions.title: Add a minimal hosted CI workflow mirroring the husky hooks
improvementOptions.description: Add a single GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) running `npm ci`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test:coverage`, and `npm run deps:check:prod` on PRs. Today the only gates are local husky hooks that fork PRs never trigger and that contributors can skip with --no-verify. No new runtime deps; reuses existing npm scripts. Optionally add a job publishing jsdoc (`npm run docs`) since docs/ is gitignored and currently unpublished.

improvementOptions.title: Add a CONTRIBUTING.md capturing the existing conventions
improvementOptions.description: The README scatters real contributor rules (centralized DOM IDs in js/constants.js, coverage thresholds, hook behavior) and the codebase has rich spec/ and plan/ conventions. Consolidate into CONTRIBUTING.md: how to run tests, the ID/constants rule, coverage gates, where specs live, and the no-build-step constraint to protect against accidental bundler additions.

verifiedBugs.title: Destructive 'Cut' with no Copy and no persistence risks losing all transcribed text
verifiedBugs.location: grabTextButton handler, lines 145-173; transcript never persisted (no localStorage write anywhere for ID.TRANSCRIPT)

verdict.title: Destructive 'Cut' with no Copy and no transcript persistence risks losing transcribed text
verdict.reasoning:
I read the faulty path directly. js/ui.js lines 146-157: the only transcript action (grabTextButton) does navigator.clipboard.writeText(text).then(() => { this.transcriptElement.value = ''; ... }) — on a successful clipboard write the textarea is unconditionally emptied. js/ui.js lines 158-163 show the clear only happens on success; on .catch the text is preserved, so one of the analyst's framings ("clipboard succeeds unexpectedly") is slightly off, but the core destructive path is real.

Persistence claim verified: STORAGE_KEYS in js/constants.js lines 37-47 has no transcript key (only model/URI/API-key/theme/device/sidebar). A codebase-wide grep for setItem/localStorage/sessionStorage shows the only writes are settings/theme/device related (js/permission-manager.js lines 118,121); nothing persists ID.TRANSCRIPT. The only transcriptElement usages (js/ui.js lines 41, 147, 151, 503-514) are in-memory DOM reads/writes, and displayTranscription (lines 502-515) only assigns to .value. So the textarea is genuinely the sole source of truth, and a reload/crash loses everything.

UI surface verified: index.html lines 134-149 contain exactly one transcript-action button (id=grab-text-button, label 'Cut') plus the textarea. There is no plain Copy, Save, Download, history, or undo control. Programmatically setting textarea.value='' also does not populate the native undo stack in standard browsers, so Ctrl+Z will not recover the cleared text.

The defect is real and confirmed as written in its facts. I downgraded severity from high to medium: the action is an explicitly labeled 'Cut' button (visible text 'Cut', aria-label 'Cut text to clipboard') that only deletes after a confirmed successful clipboard write, so the success-path deletion is intentional and user-invoked rather than a silent/accidental logic bug. The substantive risk is the total absence of transcript persistence (reload/crash data loss) and any recovery affordance, which is a genuine workflow gap for a transcription product but not a critical correctness fault.

verifiedBugs.title: Re-recording appends to prior transcription with no separation or clear-all

verdict.title: Re-recording appends to prior transcription with no per-segment labels or "clear only" control
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/ui.js displayTranscription (lines 502-515) and confirmed the literal behavior: when this.transcriptElement.value is non-empty, line 504 does `value += '

' + (text || 'No transcription returned')`, otherwise line 506 assigns it. The caller at lines 249-250 (eventBus.on(UI_TRANSCRIPTION_READY) -> displayTranscription(data.text)), fed by audio-handler.js line 361 on each completed transcription, confirms successive recordings do accumulate. The only transcript control is the "Cut" button (index.html lines 139-145; handler ui.js 145-172) which copies the whole textarea then clears it entirely (line 151 `this.transcriptElement.value = ''`); there is no per-segment "discard last" / "clear only" control. So the factual code observations in the claim are accurate.

However, this is NOT a defect. (1) The claim says "no separation" but line 504 explicitly inserts `'

'`, a blank-line separator between segments — there IS separation, just not labeled boundaries, so the claim overstates the situation. (2) The behavior is deliberate, not erroneous: the comment at line 513 ("Auto-scroll to bottom so newest text is always visible") shows accumulation is an intended design, and there is no crash, data loss, incorrect output, or broken control flow on this path. (3) The textarea at index.html line 148 is a plain editable <textarea> with no readonly attribute, so a user CAN manually select and delete any individual segment — the claim that users "cannot easily discard one bad result without losing all" is not enforced by the code. The absence of timestamps, headings, per-segment delimiters, and a dedicated non-destructive clear button is a UX/product enhancement request (a feature gap and design preference), not a code defect. Classifying as not-a-bug with low severity: legitimate as a usability improvement idea, but no faulty code path exists.

verifiedBugs.title: Pause and Cancel controls are always visible but inert when idle
verifiedBugs.location: control-buttons-container, lines 160-189; guards in recording-state-machine.js canPause 341-343 / canCancel 377-379

verdict.title: Pause and Cancel controls are always visible but inert when idle
verdict.reasoning:
I confirmed the full faulty path by reading the actual code.

VISIBILITY: In index.html, pause-button (line 161) and cancel-button (line 185) carry only class="icon-btn" with no inline display:none. Only retry-button (line 179) has style="display:none;" disabled. The container .control-buttons-container (css/styles.css 531-538) is display:flex always. .icon-btn (css/styles.css 541-561) renders a 48px circular bordered button, and .icon-btn:hover applies transform: scale(1.05) plus accent color/background. I grepped js/ and css/ and found NO code that hides .icon-btn, the container, or these two buttons at any state. So they are fully visible and hover-animated from page load onward.

INERTNESS WHEN IDLE: ui.js binds clicks to emit PAUSE_BUTTON_CLICKED / CANCEL_BUTTON_CLICKED (ui.js 128-137). audio-handler.js subscribes these to togglePause() and cancelRecording() (audio-handler.js 54-55). togglePause() (audio-handler.js 239-254) only acts if canPause() OR canResume() is true; cancelRecording() (audio-handler.js 262-267) only acts if canCancel() is true. The guards in recording-state-machine.js: canPause (341-343) returns true only in RECORDING; canResume (351-353) only in PAUSED; canCancel (377-379) only in RECORDING or PAUSED. In IDLE/ERROR (canRecord state, 330-333) all three are false, so both handlers fall through and do nothing.

STATE HANDLERS DO NOT FIX IT: The RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED handler (ui.js 200-246) for 'idle' and 'error' calls resetControlsAfterRecording() (ui.js 593-597), which only resets the timer, recording state, and pause icon via setPauseState (ui.js 580-591) — it never toggles the visibility/disabled state of pause-button or cancel-button. Contrast with retry-button, which IS correctly hidden/disabled via toggleRetryButton (ui.js 561-571).

Therefore the defect is real: two prominently styled, hover-responsive circular controls are shown in the idle/ready state but silently do nothing on click. This is a genuine affordance/clarity UX defect. Severity low is accurate — purely cosmetic/UX with no functional breakage, data loss, or security impact (the guards correctly prevent any invalid action; the only harm is misleading first-time users).

verifiedBugs.title: Raw exception messages shown directly to users in the status line

verdict.title: Raw exception messages shown directly to users in the status line
verdict.reasoning:
I read the full faulty path and confirm the claim. In js/recording-state-machine.js lines 271-280, handleErrorState builds the status text as `${MESSAGES.ERROR_PREFIX}${errorMessage}. ${MESSAGES.TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY}` (line 275). constants.js confirms ERROR_PREFIX='Error: ' (line 289) and TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY='Tap mic to retry' (line 294), so the displayed text is literally `Error: <errorMessage>. Tap mic to retry` with no category-to-guidance mapping. errorMessage is the raw value passed via data.error.

The source of errorMessage is js/audio-handler.js. In sendToAzureAPI lines 374-385, the catch block sets `rawMessage = error?.message || MESSAGES.ERROR_OCCURRED` (line 378) and returns it as result.error (line 385), only appending the network hint when isNetworkError is true (lines 379-383). That result.error is forwarded to transitionTo(ERROR, {error: result.error}) at lines 342-343, which reaches handleErrorState.

These raw messages are not user-curated. They originate in js/api-client.js _createApiError: for non-429 responses message is `API error ${status}: ${detail}` (line 182) where detail comes from server-provided JSON (`parsed?.error?.innerError?.message || parsed?.error?.message || parsed?.message`, line 131), or `API responded with status: ${status}` (line 183). For fetch failures, error.message can be a raw `Failed to fetch`/`load failed` TypeError string (matched at line 380). All of these are surfaced verbatim into the status line.

So the mechanism described in the claim is exactly correct: technical, non-curated strings (HTTP status numbers, raw server detail, fetch failure text) are shown directly to the user with no friendly mapping beyond the network hint. Severity low is appropriate: this is a single-user client app where each user supplies their own Azure credentials, so the 'internal detail leak' is bounded (no cross-user data exposure) and the strings are mostly status codes; the impact is primarily confusing/unpolished UX rather than a security problem.

verifiedBugs.title: Retry flow uses an illegal IDLE→PROCESSING transition; retry runs with no spinner, mic left enabled, and shows a raw 'Invalid state transition' error

verdict.title: Retry flow uses illegal IDLE→PROCESSING transition: retry runs with no spinner, mic left enabled, raw 'Invalid state transition' error flashed; final IDLE→IDLE also illegal
verdict.reasoning:
Verified by reading the exact code paths and confirming with runtime execution of the real RecordingStateMachine.

1. retryPendingTranscription (js/audio-handler.js:325-346): guard at 326 requires state ERROR. Line 331 does ERROR→IDLE, line 332 does IDLE→PROCESSING, line 334 calls sendToAzureAPI ignoring the boolean return of 332, line 338 does IDLE→IDLE on success.

2. Transition matrix (js/constants.js:352-361): STATE_TRANSITIONS[IDLE] = [INITIALIZING, ERROR] (line 353) — does NOT include PROCESSING; STATE_TRANSITIONS[ERROR] = [IDLE] (line 360); STATE_TRANSITIONS[PROCESSING] = [IDLE, ERROR] (line 358). So IDLE→PROCESSING and IDLE→IDLE are both illegal; only STOPPING→PROCESSING is a legal entry to PROCESSING.

3. transitionTo (js/recording-state-machine.js:86-91): on an invalid transition it calls errorHandler.handleError(new Error('Invalid state transition from idle to processing')) and returns false without running the handler.

4. errorHandler.handleError (js/error-handler.js:17-21) emits APP_EVENTS.ERROR_OCCURRED with the raw message; UI.showError listens (js/ui.js:259-262) and displays that raw string to the user.

5. handleProcessingState (js/recording-state-machine.js:228-237) emits API_REQUEST_START, UI_SPINNER_SHOW, UI_BUTTON_DISABLE_MIC — none fire because the transition failed before the handler runs.

6. canRecord (js/recording-state-machine.js:330-333) returns true in IDLE; toggleRecording (js/audio-handler.js:76-78) calls startRecordingFlow when canRecord() is true — so while the retry request is in flight and the FSM is stuck at IDLE with the mic enabled, a mic click can start a concurrent recording.

Runtime trace of the real state machine confirmed: ERROR->IDLE ok=true (state idle); IDLE->PROCESSING ok=false (state idle), logging 'ErrorOccurred: Invalid state transition from idle to processing'; IDLE->IDLE ok=false (state idle), logging 'Invalid state transition from idle to idle'. The state never leaves IDLE after the failed step, which is exactly why the test assertion at tests/audio-handler-integration.vitest.js:313 (getState()===IDLE) passes despite the bug; I ran that test and it passes (1 passed).

All claimed facts — illegal IDLE→PROCESSING, raw error flashed via ERROR_OCCURRED→UI.showError, no spinner / mic left enabled because handleProcessingState never runs, sendToAzureAPI running with FSM still IDLE, concurrent-recording hazard via canRecord, and the IDLE→IDLE silent failure making the test pass by coincidence — are independently confirmed. Severity high is appropriate: user-facing breakage on the retry path (raw error string, missing spinner, mic enabled) plus a real concurrency hazard, though it only manifests after a prior failed transcription. The proposed fix (allow ERROR→PROCESSING and go straight there, dropping the IDLE hop, and check the boolean before sending) is sound.

verifiedBugs.title: Re-clicking mic while STOPPING/CANCELLING triggers spurious 'Invalid state transition' errors and redundant stop
verifiedBugs.location: toggleRecording lines 76-82 + stopRecordingFlow lines 158-171; canInvokeStop in recording-state-machine.js:362-369

verdict.title: Re-clicking mic during STOPPING triggers a spurious 'Invalid state transition' error string and redundant stop work
verdict.reasoning:
I read the full path. toggleRecording (audio-handler.js:79-80) calls stopRecordingFlow() whenever canInvokeStop() is true. canInvokeStop (recording-state-machine.js:362-369) returns true for STOPPING and CANCELLING. stopRecordingFlow (audio-handler.js:158-171) calls transitionTo(RECORDING_STATES.STOPPING) at line 159 and ignores its boolean return — it unconditionally runs clearInterval (162), emits UI_TIMER_RESET (164), and calls safeStopRecorder() (167). transitionTo (recording-state-machine.js:86-91) validates against STATE_TRANSITIONS; constants.js:357 maps STOPPING->[PROCESSING, ERROR], so a second transition to STOPPING fails canTransitionTo, calls errorHandler.handleError with new Error('Invalid state transition from stopping to stopping') (line 89), and returns false. errorHandler.handleError (error-handler.js:17-22) emits ERROR_OCCURRED with message = error.message (the raw string). UI listens on ERROR_OCCURRED (ui.js:259-262) and calls showError(message) -> setStatus(message) (ui.js:491-493), so the raw transition error is rendered to the user's status line.

The trigger window is real and reachable for STOPPING specifically: the RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED handler for 'stopping' (ui.js:224-230) deliberately does NOT disable the mic button ('Keep button enabled during stopping state'), reinforced by recording-state-machine.js:214 ('Do not disable the mic button here'). handleStoppingState (recording-state-machine.js:202-214) only emits events and does NOT transition out of STOPPING; the move to PROCESSING happens asynchronously later in the MediaRecorder 'stop' handler (audio-handler.js:203). MIC_BUTTON_CLICKED is wired to toggleRecording (audio-handler.js:53; ui.js:125). So during the async stop flush, a second mic click re-enters stopRecordingFlow and produces the spurious error plus a redundant clearInterval/UI_TIMER_RESET/safeStopRecorder. The actual double mediaRecorder.stop() is prevented by the state !== 'inactive' guard (audio-handler.js:222-229), matching the 'no crash' claim.

One correction to the claim's scope: the CANCELLING half is NOT reachable via the mic button, because the 'cancelling' state handler calls disableMicButton() (ui.js:236-238). A disabled button cannot emit MIC_BUTTON_CLICKED, so the spurious-error path is triggerable only through STOPPING, not CANCELLING. This narrows the reproduction but does not refute the defect. Impact is cosmetic/UX: a confusing raw internal error string shown to the user plus harmless redundant teardown work; no crash, no data loss — severity low is correct.

verifiedBugs.title: Cancel can leave a stuck CANCELLING state and leaked mic stream if the MediaRecorder 'stop' event never fires
verifiedBugs.location: cancelRecording lines 262-267, stop handler lines 191-208; CANCELLING transitions in constants.js:359

verdict.title: Cancel path's cleanup lives solely in the MediaRecorder 'stop' handler with no error/timeout fallback (CANCELLING can get stuck, mic stream leaked)
verdict.reasoning: I read the exact paths. cancelRecording (audio-handler.js:262-267) only does transitionTo(CANCELLING) then safeStopRecorder(); it does not stop stream tracks or transition to IDLE itself. safeStopRecorder (lines 222-230) calls mediaRecorder.stop() only when state !== 'inactive'. ALL cancel cleanup — stream.getTracks().forEach(t=>t.stop()) (196), transitionTo(IDLE) (197), cleanup() (198) — lives exclusively inside the MediaRecorder 'stop' event handler (191-208). I confirmed via grep that the ONLY MediaRecorder listeners registered are 'dataavailable' and 'stop' (lines 187, 191); there is NO 'error' listener and NO track 'ended' listener in audio-handler.js or permission-manager.js. constants.js:359 confirms STATE_TRANSITIONS[CANCELLING] = [IDLE], so CANCELLING's only legal exit is IDLE, which is reached only inside that handler. recording-state-machine.js:377-379 confirms canCancel() is true only in RECORDING/PAUSED. So the structural claim is fully accurate: the cancel flow is single-exit and depends entirely on the 'stop' event firing, with no timeout/fallback. The fragility is real in the code. However, the trigger is unproven: in a normal cancel from RECORDING/PAUSED the recorder is 'recording'/'paused' (not 'inactive'), so stop() is called and the spec guarantees the 'stop' event fires — that path is fine. The failure only materializes if the recorder reaches 'inactive' WITHOUT having fired 'stop' (e.g. a device-unplug/track-ended error) before the user clicks cancel; then safeStopRecorder skips stop(), the handler never runs, state stays in CANCELLING, and getUserMedia tracks are never stopped (mic indicator stays on). Whether a real browser actually leaves the recorder inactive-without-stop on device removal is browser-dependent and not verifiable from the code, and the analyst conceded it is not proven against a real browser. The code-level defect (no error/timeout fallback, cleanup gated on a single event) is confirmed; the concrete crash scenario remains speculative because it hinges on unverified UA behavior plus a concurrent hardware fault. I retain severity medium because the consequence (leaked microphone / privacy indicator stuck on plus an unrecoverable UI state requiring reload) is non-trivial, but the low likelihood keeps it from being higher.

verifiedBugs.title: Resume treated as synchronous; state→RECORDING set before mediaRecorder.resume() is confirmed

verdict.title: Resume treated as synchronous; state→RECORDING set before mediaRecorder.resume() is confirmed
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/audio-handler.js togglePause (lines 239-254) and recording-state-machine.js (canResume line 351-353, transitionTo line 86-111).

The resume branch executes in this order: line 249 this.mediaRecorder.resume(); line 250 this.startTimer(); line 251 eventBus.emit(RECORDING_RESUMED); line 252 await this.stateMachine.transitionTo(RECORDING_STATES.RECORDING). Crucially, resume() is called BEFORE the FSM transition, not after.

The claim's described failure mode — "the FSM can report RECORDING while the underlying recorder is not actually capturing" because resume threw — is logically backwards. If resume() threw synchronously on line 249, execution would abort there and lines 250-252 would never run, so the FSM would REMAIN in PAUSED, not falsely report RECORDING. There is no code path where resume() throws yet the transition to RECORDING still occurs.

The claim also asserts resume() can "throw/reject." Per the W3C MediaStream Recording spec, MediaRecorder.resume() is synchronous and returns undefined (it is not Promise-based, so it cannot reject). Its only throwing case is when state is "inactive" (InvalidStateError). The guard canResume() (line 351-353) requires currentState === PAUSED, which is only reached via the canPause() branch (lines 240-244) that synchronously called mediaRecorder.pause() and transitioned to PAUSED — meaning the underlying recorder is in 'paused' state. On this happy path resume() neither throws nor silently fails: per spec a paused recorder transitions synchronously to "recording" and queues the resume event. There is no spec-defined silent-failure case that would leave the recorder not capturing while reporting success.

The pattern here is also symmetric with the pause branch (pause() called before transitionTo(PAUSED)), which is the conventional and correct ordering. Treating resume() as synchronous is correct because it IS synchronous per spec. No silent dead recording can arise from the path described.

verifiedBugs.title: whisper-translate adapter is registered and tested but unreachable from the UI

verdict.title: whisper-translate adapter is registered and tested but unreachable from the UI
verdict.reasoning: I independently read every file on the faulty path. index.html:77-80 (#model-select) and index.html:210-213 (#settings-model-select) each contain only two options: value="whisper" and value="mai-transcribe". Neither contains a whisper-translate option. js/model-adapters/index.js:6,13 imports and registers whisperTranslateModelAdapter; js/constants.js:94 defines WHISPER_TRANSLATE: 'whisper-translate'; tests/model-adapters.vitest.js:210-230 exercises it, but via createSettings(MODEL_TYPES.WHISPER_TRANSLATE), a fabricated settings object that bypasses the DOM entirely. The runtime model value comes only from getCurrentModel() (settings.js:659 -> this.modelSelect.value) and getCurrentModelFromSettings() (settings.js:669-674 -> this.settingsModelSelect.value), both feeding getModelConfig() (settings.js:682-690). Those values can only be one of the static <option> values. The persistence path cannot inject the value either: loadSavedModel (settings.js:101-104) does select.value = savedModel, which is a no-op for a value with no matching option, and STORAGE_KEYS.MODEL is written only by saveSettings (settings.js:605) from getCurrentModelFromSettings(), itself option-limited. A repo-wide grep for whisper-translate/WHISPER_TRANSLATE/whisperTranslate found zero code paths that ever assign 'whisper-translate' to a select. Therefore the adapter is genuinely unreachable from the UI: the translate feature is dead at runtime. I lowered severity from medium to low because there is no incorrect behavior, crash, data loss, or security impact for the flows that ARE wired up; the only impact is an advertised/maintained capability that silently never ships plus the maintenance cost of tested-but-dead code.

verifiedBugs.title: Public parseResponse() result depends on registry insertion order, not the active model

verdict.title: Public parseResponse() result depends on registry insertion order, not the active model
verdict.reasoning:
I read every path the claim depends on and confirm it.

1) js/api-client.js:245-256 — parseResponse(data) loops `for (const adapter of this.adapterRegistry.values())` and returns `adapter.parseResponse(data)` from the first adapter that does not throw, ignoring the active model entirely. The inline comment at line 246 even states it "keeps the legacy cross-shape parser behavior."

2) js/api-client.js:49-91 — transcribe() is, as claimed, model-strict: line 51 resolves the adapter from config.model via _getModelAdapter, and line 78 calls only that active adapter's parseResponse. So the order-coupling does NOT affect the production transcribe path.

3) js/model-adapters/index.js:10-14 — the registry Map is constructed in order [mai, whisper, whisper-translate], with a comment (line 9) "Order matters for AzureAPIClient.parseResponse(): adapters are tried in legacy parse precedence." Map iteration preserves this insertion order, so MAI is tried first.

4) js/model-adapters/response-parsers.js:7-33 — parseWhisperResponse only handles a trimmed string or `data.text`. parseMaiTranscribeResponse handles a string, then `data.combinedPhrases` (lines 24-26), then `data.text` (lines 28-29). Both accept `{text}`, so the shapes overlap. A payload with BOTH combinedPhrases and text matches MAI's combinedPhrases branch first; whisper is never reached because MAI is first in the Map.

5) tests/model-adapters.vitest.js:188-196 — with active model WHISPER, the test asserts parseResponse({combinedPhrases:[{text:'From combinedPhrases'}], text:'From text field'}) returns 'From combinedPhrases' (the MAI interpretation), proving the public helper is model-agnostic and order-coupled exactly as described.

The defect is real: the public parseResponse is order-coupled and model-agnostic, and a future 4th provider overlapping {text} could be silently mis-parsed depending on Map insertion position. Severity is correctly low: the live transcribe() path is unaffected (active-adapter strict), real Whisper/MAI responses have disjoint shapes in practice, and the only current caller of the ambiguous dual-shape case is the test itself. This is documented/intentional legacy behavior rather than an active production failure, but the analyst's code reading and the latent maintainability hazard are accurate, so I confirm it at low severity.

verifiedBugs.title: No request timeout or AbortController on transcription fetch

verdict.title: No request timeout or AbortController on transcription fetch
verdict.reasoning:
I read the full call chain and confirmed the defect.

js/api-client.js lines 64-68: the fetch options object is built as { method: HTTP_METHODS.POST, headers, body } with no `signal` property. js/api-client.js line 139: `const response = await fetch(uri, options);` passes that bare options object, so no AbortSignal is attached. A repo-wide grep for AbortController/AbortSignal/Promise.race returned zero matches in source; the only setTimeout uses are UI status (ui.js, status-helper.js), settings hover/init timers, and the retry backoff `_sleep` (api-client.js:224). None of these abort or time out a hung request.

js/api-client.js line 141: the retry condition only evaluates `response.status` against RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES. That code runs only after `fetch` resolves with a Response. A connection that opens but never delivers a response never resolves the fetch promise, so the `await` at line 139 blocks forever and the for-loop (line 138) cannot advance to a retry. Retry therefore cannot rescue a stalled (no-status) connection — exactly as claimed.

Callers confirm the stuck-UI consequence. js/audio-handler.js:354 does `await this.apiClient.transcribe(...)` inside sendToAzureAPI; its catch (line 374) and the finally that hides the spinner (lines 386-388) only execute once the awaited promise settles. recording-state-machine.js handleProcessingState (line 228) shows the spinner and disables the mic button. If fetch hangs, the await never returns, so PROCESSING never transitions to ERROR/IDLE — spinner stays, mic stays disabled, no recovery without a page reload. The navigator.onLine===false guard (audio-handler.js:350) only catches a browser already-offline state before the request, not a connection that opens then stalls.

The adapters (js/model-adapters/whisper.js etc.) only build request payloads and perform no fetch, so _fetchWithRetry is the single, correct, provider-agnostic place to add a timeout/AbortController — matching the analyst's note.

Severity adjusted to medium (kept): browsers impose their own very long network-level timeout (~minutes), so it is not literally infinite in every environment, and the failure is a recoverable stuck UI (reload) on genuinely stalled connections rather than data loss or a security issue. But the absence of any application-level timeout is real and the UX failure mode is exactly as described.

verifiedBugs.title: Unhandled localStorage exceptions cause torn writes in saveSettings

verdict.title: Unhandled localStorage exceptions cause torn writes in saveSettings
verdict.reasoning: I read js/settings.js saveSettings (585-650) directly. There is no try/catch around any setItem. Line 605 writes STORAGE_KEYS.MODEL first; lines 607-611 write the credential pair (URI + API key) for the active model; line 618 closeSettingsModal() and lines 620-649 (success status, SETTINGS_SAVED, SETTINGS_MODEL_CHANGED, SETTINGS_LOADED, SETTINGS_UPDATED) all run only after the writes. I confirmed the click handler at lines 178-180 calls saveSettings() with no surrounding try/catch, and grep found no window.onerror or unhandledrejection handlers in js/. The only try/catch in the file (564-569) is inside getValidationErrors, unrelated to localStorage. Therefore, if a setItem on the credential write throws (QuotaExceededError on full storage; SecurityError in some private-browsing modes), the exception propagates unhandled: MODEL was already persisted, credentials were not, closeSettingsModal never runs (frozen modal), and no success/error feedback is emitted. I verified the persisted MODEL key is consumed on the next page load via loadSavedModel (line 101) and getModelConfig/getCurrentModel (658, 682-690), so a torn write yields a genuinely inconsistent persisted state on reload (new model selected, credentials for it missing/stale). The mechanism is real and matches the description. Two corrections that lower severity rather than disprove the bug: (1) The description's headline trigger, Safari private browsing, actually throws on the FIRST setItem (line 605, MODEL), which produces a frozen modal but NOT a torn write since nothing is persisted; a true torn write requires QuotaExceededError to land specifically on the credential write after the MODEL write succeeded — plausible but a narrow case. (2) In-session functional impact is limited because runtime credential reads use the live this.modelSelect.value (getCurrentModel, line 658), not the persisted MODEL key, so the inconsistency mainly surfaces after a reload; no previously-saved credentials are destroyed. Given the niche trigger and bounded practical impact, I downgrade severity from medium to low, but the defect itself is confirmed.

verifiedBugs.title: loadSettingsToForm leaves stale credentials in inputs when target model has no saved value
