verdict.title: loadSettingsToForm leaves stale credentials in inputs when target model has no saved value
verdict.reasoning:
I read loadSettingsToForm (js/settings.js:459-488), the constructor DOM caching (lines 47-52), the modal-selector change handler (lines 142-159), updateSettingsVisibility (lines 413-422), openSettingsModal (lines 431-437), _getActiveInputs (lines 497-503), and saveSettings (lines 585-612), plus index.html lines 226-240 and the ID constants in js/constants.js (lines 170-173).

The claim's core premise is that switching the modal selector to MAI could make "the MAI key field retain a previously shown" Whisper value, leading save to write a stale value under the wrong model key. This is false because Whisper and MAI use entirely separate DOM input elements: whisperKeyInput is the element with id 'whisper-key' (index.html:227) and maiTranscribeKeyInput is the distinct element with id 'mai-transcribe-key' (index.html:240). loadSettingsToForm populates each from its own dedicated storage key (lines 471-473 from WHISPER_API_KEY, lines 480-481 from MAI_TRANSCRIBE_API_KEY). No code anywhere copies a Whisper value into the MAI element; grep for these symbols shows only the per-model assignments.

Switching the selector (lines 143-158) only calls updateSettingsVisibility, which toggles display of two separate sections (lines 416-421). It does NOT move the Whisper value into the MAI field; the Whisper value stays in the hidden whisper-key input, and the mai-transcribe-key input remains whatever it was (empty if no MAI key was ever loaded/typed). So the MAI field correctly shows empty.

The save path (lines 599-612) reads from _getActiveInputs() (lines 497-503), which returns the model-matched element, and writes strictly to the active model's storage keys. A Whisper value in the Whisper input can therefore never be written under the MAI storage key. The "stale value under the wrong model key" failure cannot occur via any in-app path.

The truthy-guard-with-no-else pattern the analyst cites is real, but its only consequence would be a same-field stale value if a model's stored credential were cleared out-of-band while the cached input still held the old value (e.g., manual localStorage edit). No code path in the app clears a stored credential without also resetting the field, so this is a theoretical/defensive concern, not a concrete confirmed defect. The described cross-model leakage does not happen. Hence not-a-bug, severity none.

verifiedBugs.title: validateConfiguration is dead code; only getValidationErrors is wired to save

verdict.title: validateConfiguration is dead code; only getValidationErrors is wired to save
verdict.reasoning: I read js/settings.js:518-619 and grepped all *.js, *.html, and *.md files. Verified facts: (1) validateConfiguration() is defined at lines 527-534 — it calls getValidationErrors(), and on errors emits APP_EVENTS.SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR (line 530) and returns false, else returns true. (2) grep -rn "validateConfiguration" --include="*.js" shows references ONLY at its own definition site (settings.js lines 524 doc-comment, 527 declaration) and in tests/settings-unit.vitest.js. There is no production caller anywhere in ./js. (3) The only UI-wired validation path is saveSettings(): the save button listener at lines 178-180 calls this.saveSettings(); saveSettings (line 585) independently calls getValidationErrors() at line 588 and emits SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR itself at line 590 — it never delegates to validateConfiguration(). (4) grep for subscribers to SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR in ./js found no eventBus.on(...VALIDATION...) handler, so no non-save consumer reacts to the event either. Therefore the documented public method validateConfiguration never executes in production; it is dead code and a duplicate of the save-path validation. The bug is real as a maintainability/dead-code defect. It is not a runtime failure (nothing breaks at runtime), so severity stays low. The claim's speculative phrasing about "any consumer relying on it gets nothing" is moot since there are no such consumers, but that does not affect the confirmed core: two parallel validation entry points, one never invoked.

verifiedBugs.title: whisper-translate model is registered but not selectable, so its config is unreachable

verdict.title: whisper-translate adapter is registered but has no UI selector option, making its config path unreachable
verdict.reasoning:
I independently confirmed every link in the chain.

1) Registry: js/model-adapters/index.js lines 5-14 import whisperTranslateModelAdapter and register it as the third entry of modelAdapterRegistry alongside maiTranscribe and whisper.

2) Adapter definition: js/model-adapters/whisper-translate.js lines 8-26 define a fully-built adapter (id MODEL_TYPES.WHISPER_TRANSLATE = 'whisper-translate' per constants.js line 94) with storageKeys.apiKey = STORAGE_KEYS.WHISPER_API_KEY and storageKeys.uri = STORAGE_KEYS.WHISPER_URI — identical sharing to whisper.js lines 11-14. So it is real and intends to reuse the Whisper credentials, exactly as claimed.

3) UI selectors are static and offer only two options: index.html #model-select (lines 77-80) lists only value="whisper" and value="mai-transcribe"; #settings-model-select (lines 210-213) lists the same two. There is no 'whisper-translate' option.

4) Options are never added dynamically. grep across js/ shows createElement('option')/appendChild only for inputDeviceSelect (settings.js lines 389-401), never for the model selects. getCurrentModel() (settings.js lines 658-659) simply returns this.modelSelect.value, which can only be 'whisper' or 'mai-transcribe'.

5) Selection/config logic has no concept of translate. The settings layer uses strictly binary isMai branching (settings.js lines 415, 498, 550, 599, 684) where the non-MAI branch always maps to WHISPER_* keys; nothing references WHISPER_TRANSLATE outside the adapter file, the registry import, and constants.js. grep confirms 'whisper-translate'/WHISPER_TRANSLATE appears only in those three places.

6) The API client (api-client.js lines 49-51) resolves the adapter via config.model from getModelConfig() (settings.js lines 682-689), which derives model from getCurrentModel() — i.e. the dropdown value. Since the dropdown can never yield 'whisper-translate', _getModelAdapter is never called with that id.

Conclusion: the adapter and its config path are genuinely dead from the UI. One minor correction to the analyst's wording: even manually setting transcription_model='whisper-translate' in localStorage would not reliably activate it, because loadSavedModel (settings.js line 102) assigns it to a <select> that has no matching <option>, so the element coerces back to 'whisper' and getCurrentModel() returns 'whisper'. This makes the dead code slightly more dead than claimed, but does not change the verdict. Severity low is appropriate: no user-facing breakage, just unreachable/dead code and an inconsistent per-model config model.

verifiedBugs.title: Asymmetric API-key format validation between Whisper (strict) and MAI (none)

verdict.title: Asymmetric API-key format validation between Whisper (strict 32-hex) and MAI (none)
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/settings.js getValidationErrors (lines 543-558) and confirmed the asymmetry exactly as claimed. Line 553 applies API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN to all keys; line 555 adds `else if (!isMai && !/^[A-F0-9]{32}$/i.test(apiKey))` which gates ONLY non-MAI (Whisper / whisper-translate) keys behind a strict 32-char-hex regex, pushing 'Invalid API key format'. MAI keys (isMai true) skip this branch entirely. The comment on line 556 ("Speech keys are longer alphanumeric") itself acknowledges that non-32-hex Azure key shapes exist.

I confirmed API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN in js/constants.js:76 is `/^[\x21-\x7E]+$/` — visible ASCII only, no length/charset constraint — so it would not catch a longer alphanumeric key; the 32-hex regex is the sole format gate on the Whisper path. I also checked js/api-client.js:283, the runtime validation, which applies only API_KEY_VALUE_PATTERN with no format-specific check, so the settings modal's getValidationErrors is indeed the blocking gate (saveSettings at lines 588-597 returns early on any error, blocking save). There is no override/escape hatch.

The over-strict, hard-coded regex with no escape hatch is read directly from source and is real. However, whether it actually rejects a VALID key and blocks a real user depends on the external assumption that Azure issues a Whisper-endpoint key that is not 32-char hex. Current Azure Speech/Whisper resource keys are 32-char hex, so today the check validates correctly and causes no failure. Because the user-blocking outcome rests on a future/external Azure key-format assumption I cannot independently verify, the claim stays at "likely" rather than "confirmed." Severity low is appropriate: no impact on current functionality, MAI path unaffected, and it is a forward-compatibility/robustness concern rather than an active defect.

verifiedBugs.title: No request timeout/abort leaves state machine stuck in PROCESSING on a hung request

verdict.title: No request timeout/abort leaves state machine stuck in PROCESSING on a hung request
verdict.reasoning:
I independently traced the full faulty path by reading the code.

1. api-client.js:137-162 `_fetchWithRetry` calls `const response = await fetch(uri, options)` at line 139. The `options` object is built in `transcribe` at lines 64-68 as `{ method, headers, body }` — no `signal` field. A repo-wide grep (`AbortController|abort|signal|setTimeout|fetch(` over js/, excluding tests) found the only `fetch` at line 139 and no AbortController/signal anywhere. The model adapters' `buildRequest` (mai-transcribe.js, whisper.js, whisper-translate.js) do not inject a signal either. So the fetch has no abort path and no timeout.

2. The await chain that hangs if fetch never settles: audio-handler.js:204 `await this.processAndSendAudio(stream)` -> :310 `await this.sendToAzureAPI(audioBlob)` -> :354 `await this.apiClient.transcribe(...)` -> api-client.js:64 `await this._fetchWithRetry(...)` -> :139 `await fetch(...)`.

3. If fetch never resolves (a silent network stall after connection, no TCP reset/error), the promise never settles. `sendToAzureAPI`'s catch (374-385) and finally (386-388, which emits UI_SPINNER_HIDE) never run. `processAndSendAudio` (310-322) never reaches its IDLE/ERROR transition, so the machine stays in PROCESSING.

4. recording-state-machine.js handleProcessingState (228-237) already emitted UI_BUTTON_DISABLE_MIC (235) and UI_SPINNER_SHOW (236). With no exit, the mic stays disabled and the spinner stays visible. There is no cancel control during PROCESSING (handleCancellingState is only reachable from RECORDING, not PROCESSING). Recovery requires a page reload. The same hang affects the retry path (retryPendingTranscription:334).

The mechanism is real and matches the claim. The proposed fix (AbortController + bounded timeout producing an AbortError) would be caught by the existing catch at 374 and surfaced as an error, freeing the state machine.

Severity adjusted from high to medium: the trigger is a narrow condition (a silent stall mid-response — a hard failure instead throws TypeError, which the catch at 379-380 already handles as a network error). Impact is a recoverable stuck UI (reload fixes it), not data loss, corruption, or security exposure. Browser/OS socket idle timeouts may eventually settle the fetch, though not promptly or reliably. Genuine usability defect, but bounded blast radius, so medium.

verifiedBugs.title: recorder stop() failure emits RECORDING_ERROR that no listener consumes, stranding STOPPING state
verifiedBugs.location: stopRecordingFlow (166-171) and safeStopRecorder (222-230); event has no consumer (grep: RECORDING_ERROR only emitted in audio-handler.js:169,227 and recording-state-machine.js:273)

verdict.title: recorder stop() failure emits RECORDING_ERROR with no consumer, stranding the state machine in STOPPING
verdict.reasoning:
I read the full faulty path and confirm the defect, with one mechanism correction to the analyst's account.

RECORDING_ERROR has no consumer: grep across js/ shows it is only ever emitted (audio-handler.js:169, audio-handler.js:227, recording-state-machine.js:273) and the event-bus definition (event-bus.js:246). There is no eventBus.on(APP_EVENTS.RECORDING_ERROR, ...) anywhere — ui.js listeners (lines 185-372) and settings.js never subscribe to it. So emitting RECORDING_ERROR does nothing functional; it does not drive any state transition or UI reset.

Stranding in STOPPING: stopRecordingFlow (audio-handler.js:158-171) transitions to STOPPING (line 159) then calls safeStopRecorder. STATE_TRANSITIONS[STOPPING] = [PROCESSING, ERROR] (constants.js:357). The only exit to PROCESSING is the MediaRecorder 'stop' event handler (audio-handler.js:191-208) calling transitionTo(PROCESSING) at line 203. If mediaRecorder.stop() throws, that 'stop' event never fires, and nothing else transitions to ERROR. The machine is left permanently in STOPPING. handleStoppingState (recording-state-machine.js:202-216) and ui.js:224-230 both deliberately keep the mic button enabled in STOPPING. A re-click routes through toggleRecording -> canInvokeStop (includes STOPPING, recording-state-machine.js:362-369) -> stopRecordingFlow -> transitionTo(STOPPING), which fails canTransitionTo (STOPPING not reachable from STOPPING), logs an invalid-transition error and returns false, then re-runs safeStopRecorder which re-fails. So there is no automatic recovery short of page reload.

Correction to the analyst's mechanism: the claim states both audio-handler.js:169 and :227 fire and that the outer try/catch in stopRecordingFlow (lines 166-170) handles the error. That is inaccurate. safeStopRecorder (lines 222-230) has its OWN try/catch that swallows stop()'s exception and emits RECORDING_ERROR at line 227; it never re-throws. Therefore the outer try/catch at 166-170 is effectively dead code for this path and line 169 does not fire — only line 227 fires. This is a minor mischaracterization that does not affect the conclusion: the orphaned RECORDING_ERROR + stranded STOPPING state is real.

Severity: medium is fair but the trigger is narrow. MediaRecorder.stop() per spec throws InvalidStateError only when state === 'inactive', which the guard at line 223 (state !== 'inactive') already excludes, so in normal browsers stop() rarely throws here. When it does (engine quirks/disposed stream), the consequence is a permanently stuck UI ('Finishing...' status) needing a reload. Real defect, low-to-medium real-world likelihood, medium impact.

verifiedBugs.title: Retry button offered for permanently non-retryable errors (401/403/400/bad config)
verifiedBugs.location: processAndSendAudio (306-323), retryPendingTranscription (325-346); canRetry derived at lines 320,344

verdict.title: Retry button offered for permanently non-retryable errors (401/403/400/bad config)
verdict.reasoning:
I read every load-bearing path and confirm the defect.

1) audio-handler.js:308 sets `this.pendingRetryBlob = audioBlob` unconditionally as soon as audio is captured in processAndSendAudio.

2) It is cleared ONLY on success (line 315 in processAndSendAudio; line 336 in retryPendingTranscription) or at the start of a fresh recording (line 102 in startRecordingFlow). There is no failure path that clears it.

3) sendToAzureAPI's catch block (lines 374-388) calls this.cleanup() and returns { success: false, error: errorMessage }. I read cleanup() (lines 397-416): it resets timerInterval, audioChunks, recordingStartTime, and mediaRecorder, but never touches pendingRetryBlob. So after ANY transcription error the blob persists.

4) Consequently `canRetry: Boolean(this.pendingRetryBlob)` at lines 320 and 344 evaluates to true for every error — including 401, 403, and 400 — which are deterministic and will fail identically on retry.

5) ui.js:240 (`this.toggleRetryButton(Boolean(data.canRetry))` in the 'error' state case) shows the retry button whenever canRetry is true, so the misleading affordance reaches the user.

6) The status code needed to gate this correctly is produced upstream: api-client.js:186-191 attaches error.apiContext = { status, details, retryAfter }. But sendToAzureAPI's catch discards it, returning only the string errorMessage, so processAndSendAudio has no way to distinguish retryable from non-retryable errors and decides purely on blob presence. (Note: RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES at api-client.js:12 is used only for the internal _fetchWithRetry transient-retry loop on 429/5xx and never reaches the user-facing button — consistent with the claim that the distinguishing info exists but is unused for the button.)

Result: a user with a bad key (401), forbidden access (403), or malformed/unsupported audio (400) is shown a Retry button that re-sends the identical blob with the same broken config, guaranteeing the same failure and wasting an API call. This is a real UX/correctness defect. Severity medium is correct: misleading affordance + wasted requests, but no crash or data loss. A partial mitigation exists for locally-missing/invalid keys (validateConfig emits API_CONFIG_MISSING to open settings), but it does NOT cover a server-rejected-yet-syntactically-valid key (true 401) nor 400/403, so the core claim stands.

verifiedBugs.title: _fetchWithRetry can fall through to return undefined if loop invariant changes

verdict.title: _fetchWithRetry cannot return undefined on any currently reachable path
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/api-client.js lines 137-162 (the function) and lines 11-12 (the constants: MAX_TRANSCRIPTION_RETRIES = 5, RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES = new Set([429,500,502,503,504])), plus the consumption at lines 64-72.

The loop at line 138 iterates attempt = 0..MAX_TRANSCRIPTION_RETRIES inclusive (6 iterations). The return guard at line 141 is `response.ok || !RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES.has(response.status) || attempt === MAX_TRANSCRIPTION_RETRIES`. On the final iteration attempt === MAX_TRANSCRIPTION_RETRIES is always true, so line 142 unconditionally returns the response. Every loop exit goes through line 142; there is no `break`. Therefore control can never fall off the end of the loop, and the function provably always returns a Response object. The `undefined` return is dead/unreachable code on every current path.

The claim itself concedes this ("undefined is unreachable today"). Its argument rests entirely on a hypothetical future edit to the loop bound, the guard, or the constant. That is a defensive-coding / maintainability observation, not a presently-reachable defect. The described failure at line 70 (`Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'ok')`) cannot occur today because response is always a Response.

Per the skeptical standard, a defect that requires an unwritten future edit to manifest is not a live bug. The code is correct as written; adding a trailing throw would only improve readability/robustness. Hence not-a-bug, severity none. (A reviewer could legitimately note it as a minor style nit, but it does not constitute a defect.)

verifiedBugs.title: Decode/empty-audio failures categorized as generic errors with no specific message
verifiedBugs.location: convertToWav (16-25), invoked from mai-transcribe.js:22 inside transcribe try (api-client.js:54)

verdict.title: Decode/empty-audio failures surfaced as raw EncodingError with no specific message and a futile retry
verdict.reasoning:
I read every step of the claimed path.

1) audio-converter.js:16-25: convertToWav has no try/catch around `const audioBuffer = await audioContext.decodeAudioData(arrayBuffer);` (line 20). An empty or undecodable blob makes decodeAudioData reject with a browser DOMException whose name is "EncodingError" (e.g. "Unable to decode audio data").

2) model-adapters/mai-transcribe.js:22: buildRequest calls `const wavBlob = await convertToWav(audioBlob);`. buildRequest is awaited inside transcribe's try at api-client.js:54, so the rejection enters the try, is caught at api-client.js:87 (_handleApiError logs/emits), and re-thrown at line 89.

3) audio-handler.js:374-385: sendToAzureAPI's catch computes isNetworkError = `error?.name === 'TypeError' || /network|failed to fetch|load failed/i.test(rawMessage)`. An EncodingError has name "EncodingError" (not TypeError) and a message that does not match the regex, so errorMessage = rawMessage (the raw decode text). navigator.onLine===true so the early CHECK_INTERNET throw (line 350) does not fire.

4) recording-state-machine.js:271-277 handleErrorState emits `${MESSAGES.ERROR_PREFIX}${errorMessage}. ${MESSAGES.TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY}`. With ERROR_PREFIX 'Error: ' and TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY 'Tap mic to retry' (constants.js:289,294), the user sees `Error: <raw EncodingError text>. Tap mic to retry`.

5) Retry is futile: audio-handler.js:308 sets pendingRetryBlob = audioBlob before sending; on failure processAndSendAudio (line 318-321) goes to ERROR with canRetry true. retryPendingTranscription (line 325-334) re-sends the same pendingRetryBlob, re-running convertToWav on the same bad blob, failing identically.

6) No specific message exists: I read the full MESSAGES object (constants.js:239-297) — no decode/empty/too-short/no-audio entry.

7) No length/size guard: the MediaRecorder 'stop' handler (audio-handler.js:191-204) unconditionally transitions to PROCESSING and calls processAndSendAudio, which builds the blob from this.audioChunks (line 306-307) with no length or byte-size check.

All seven sub-claims are verified by reading the actual code. Severity low is correct: it is an error-messaging/UX quality defect (confusing raw browser error plus a retry that cannot succeed for that blob), not a crash or data-loss bug, and it requires an unusual input (instant tap-stop or a corrupt/empty recording). Line numbers cited by the analyst match the current source.

verifiedBugs.title: Synchronous WAV encoding blocks the main thread for long MAI-Transcribe recordings

verdict.title: Synchronous WAV encode loop in encodeWav can briefly freeze the main thread for long MAI-Transcribe recordings
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/audio-converter.js in full and js/model-adapters/mai-transcribe.js. A real but partial defect exists, and the analyst's description is materially inaccurate on scope and file path.

CONFIRMED core: encodeWav (lines 116-121) is a genuine synchronous main-thread loop that iterates every PCM sample (Math.max/min + DataView.setInt16) with no yielding. It is called synchronously at line 23 from convertToWav, which is awaited at mai-transcribe.js line 22 inside buildRequest. With TARGET_SAMPLE_RATE=16000 and NUM_CHANNELS=1 (lines 6-8), a 5-minute recording yields ~4.8M iterations. I benchmarked the exact loop body in V8: ~204 ms for 4.8M samples on fast hardware; a browser on mid/low-end hardware would be 2-5x slower. There is no recording duration cap (only one setInterval in audio-handler.js, a timer display), so multi-minute recordings are realistic. This is MAI-only: whisper.js (lines 16-18) appends the raw blob with no conversion.

REFUTED / OVERSTATED claims by the analyst:
1. Wrong file path: the caller is js/model-adapters/mai-transcribe.js line 22, not js/mai-transcribe.js (that file does not exist).
2. The central claim that decodeAudioData (line 20) AND the OfflineAudioContext resample render (startRendering, line 52) execute synchronously on the main thread is FALSE. Both return Promises and run off the main thread; the awaits at lines 17, 20, 21 yield to the event loop, so the onProgress(CONVERTING_AUDIO) status set at mai-transcribe.js line 18 CAN paint during those gaps. The only true synchronous main-thread blocker is encodeWav (and downmixToMono lines 61-78, which short-circuits to a no-op for the common mono case via lines 62-63 since the OfflineAudioContext is created with NUM_CHANNELS=1).

So the failure mechanism the analyst describes ("the whole page is frozen during decode + resample + downmix + encode") does not occur. Only the final encode stage blocks, for roughly a few hundred ms even on a long recording. The defect is real but its impact is smaller and its mechanism narrower than claimed, so I downgrade classification to likely and severity to low.

verifiedBugs.title: Visualization redraws full canvas at RAF rate despite 100ms data cadence
verifiedBugs.location: draw() RAF callback (112-151) vs sampler setInterval (104-110)

verdict.title: Visualization redraws full canvas at RAF rate despite 100ms data cadence
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/visualization.js in full and ui.js:305-334 (the only caller). The claim is an efficiency observation, not a correctness bug, and every factual assertion checks out.

1. Per-frame full clear: lines 115-116 set fillStyle=bgColor and call fillRect(0,0,canvas.width,canvas.height) unconditionally at the top of the draw() RAF callback (registered via requestAnimationFrame at line 113). Confirmed.

2. Per-frame full bar re-render: the loop at lines 124-151 iterates over totalBars (=amplitudeHistory.length) and draws every visible bar (beginPath/roundRect/fill at 144-150) on every frame. Confirmed.

3. Data cadence: the ONLY per-frame mutator of amplitudeHistory during the draw loop is the setInterval at 104-110, which pushes/shifts one sample every SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS=100 (line 12). The grep confirmed no other code path mutates amplitudeHistory except updateCanvasSize (61-63), which only fires on the occasional resize event, not per frame. So between sampler ticks the buffer is constant.

4. Pixel-identical frames: I verified there is NO time-based or frame-counter-based state in draw(). Every quantity feeding the rendered pixels (amplitude=amplitudeHistory[i] line 125, centerY 118, maxHalfHeight 119, fadeZone 120, x 127, alpha 133-137) derives purely from amplitudeHistory contents and canvas dimensions. The left-edge fade at 135-136 is positional (x-based), not time-based. Therefore between two sampler ticks the output is genuinely pixel-identical, so at ~60fps vs 10Hz data roughly 5 of every 6 frames are redundant redraws. Confirmed.

5. Allocation-free loop: confirmed — fillStyles strings are pre-built once at 94-98 and quantize() (99) just indexes the array; no per-bar string concat or object allocation. So the cost is wasted canvas fill/draw work, not GC pressure.

6. The proposed dirty-flag gate is sound and would cut redraw work ~6x with no visual change, precisely because nothing varies between samples.

Severity correctly low: this only runs while recording, the work is bounded by maxBars, and it is wasted CPU/GPU cycles rather than any correctness failure, leak, or user-visible defect. I found no missed reason the per-frame redraw is actually required, so the claim cannot be refuted.

verifiedBugs.title: Settings modal has no focus trap, initial focus, or focus restoration

verdict.title: Settings modal has no focus trap, initial focus, or focus restoration
verdict.reasoning: I read js/settings.js:431-450 directly. openSettingsModal() (431-437) only calls loadSettingsToForm(), updateSettingsVisibility(), sets this.settingsModal.style.display='block', and emits UI_SETTINGS_OPENED. It never calls .focus() on any element inside the dialog and never records the previously-focused element. closeSettingsModal() (446-450) only sets display='none' and emits UI_SETTINGS_CLOSED; it never restores focus to #settings-button. index.html:200 confirms the modal is a plain <div id="settings-modal" class="modal" role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby="modal-title"> — NOT a native <dialog> opened via showModal(), so the browser provides no built-in focus trapping or background inerting. A repo-wide grep for keydown/focus/Tab/inert/aria-hidden showed: the only keydown handler (_panelEscHandler, settings.js:281-292) is scoped solely to the sidebar (pinned / hover-preview), does nothing for the modal, and never intercepts Tab; the only other .focus() (ui.js:509) targets the transcript element, unrelated; no inert or aria-hidden is applied to background content when the modal opens. Therefore: (a) on open, keyboard focus remains on the invoking button outside the dialog; (b) Tab is never trapped, so it walks into the still-focusable background controls behind the backdrop; (c) on close, focus is not deterministically restored. This directly violates the declared aria-modal=true contract and WCAG 2.4.3. The claim is real and confirmed. I lowered severity from high to medium: it is an accessibility-only defect affecting an optional settings dialog, with no data loss or functional breakage, though high is defensible given the explicit aria-modal contract being actively violated.

verifiedBugs.title: Settings modal cannot be closed with the Escape key

verdict.title: Settings modal cannot be closed with the Escape key
verdict.reasoning: I read the faulty path directly. The sole keydown listener in the codebase is _panelEscHandler (js/settings.js:281-292), registered at line 291 (document.addEventListener('keydown', ...)) and torn down at line 726. Inside it (lines 282-288), the Escape branch only calls this.unpinSidebar() (pinned sidebar) or this._hideHoverPreview() (hover-preview sidebar); it never touches the settings modal or closeSettingsModal(). grep across js/ confirms this is the only key-event listener anywhere (only one addEventListener matching "key"). The settings modal is a plain div, not a native dialog: index.html:200 is <div id="settings-modal" class="modal" role="dialog" aria-modal="true">, and grep confirms there is no <dialog> tag, no .showModal(), and no HTMLDialogElement usage anywhere — so there is no browser-native Escape dismissal. The modal is opened/closed purely by toggling style.display (openSettingsModal sets display:'block' at js/settings.js:434; closeSettingsModal sets display:'none' at line 447). The only wired close paths are the × close-modal button (js/settings.js:167-169) and a click on the modal container/.modal-backdrop (js/settings.js:171-175). Therefore Escape does nothing while the dialog is open, and if the sidebar happens to be open underneath, Escape would even dismiss the sidebar instead. This violates the WAI-ARIA modal-dialog dismissal convention. I downgraded severity from high to medium because the dialog is not a trap: it remains dismissable via the keyboard-focusable × button (aria-label="Close settings dialog", index.html:205) and the backdrop, so the impact is a convention/usability gap rather than a lockout.

verifiedBugs.title: Validation errors are shown outside the open dialog and never announced in-context

verdict.title: Validation errors are shown outside the open dialog and never announced in-context
verdict.reasoning: I read the faulty path and every dependency. js/settings.js:588-597: when getValidationErrors() returns a non-empty array, saveSettings() emits SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR and UI_STATUS_UPDATE (message: errors[0] || MESSAGES.FILL_REQUIRED_FIELDS) then returns. closeSettingsModal() is only called on the success path at line 618, so on error the modal stays open. grep across js/ and index.html shows SETTINGS_VALIDATION_ERROR has only emit sites (settings.js:530, 590) and the enum/doc in event-bus.js:223,293 — there is no eventBus.on consumer, so the structured errors array is dropped. The only UI_STATUS_UPDATE listener is js/ui.js:185, which routes temporary messages to this.statusElement = getElementById(ID.STATUS); ID.STATUS = 'status' (constants.js:154). That #status element is at index.html:157 inside the .controls-area card, outside the #settings-modal subtree (index.html:200-246). CSS (styles.css:730-758) gives .modal position:fixed; z-index:1000; inset:0 and .modal-backdrop a backdrop-filter blur over the whole page, so #status is rendered behind the modal backdrop while the dialog is open. The modal markup contains no error container and no role=alert, and showTemporaryStatus (status-helper.js) only mutates the external #status element, whose aria-live is polite (not assertive). Thus a keyboard/SR user focused inside the open dialog gets no in-context error feedback. Every claimed assertion checks out. The claim that only errors[0] reaches #status is also accurate since the branch only runs when errors.length>0. Severity medium is appropriate: this is a UX/accessibility defect (message hidden behind backdrop, dropped structured errors, no in-dialog announcement), not a crash or data loss — validation still blocks the save.

verifiedBugs.title: Primary live-region status text fails WCAG AA contrast in both themes

verdict.title: Primary live-region status text fails WCAG AA contrast in both themes
verdict.reasoning:
I independently verified every load-bearing element of this claim by reading the files and recomputing the WCAG relative-luminance contrast ratios.

Tokens (css/styles.css): light --text-muted #9CA3AF (line 16), dark --text-muted #6B7294 (line 55); --bg-surface light #FFFFFF (line 10) / dark #151929 (line 49); --bg-inset light #F5F6FC (line 11) / dark #1C2137 (line 50); --accent light #5B6EF5 (line 17); --bg-primary light #EEF0FB (line 9).

Status region: index.html:157 is the single aria-live="polite" region, <div id="status" class="status">. .status (styles.css:508-515) sets color:var(--text-muted), font-size:0.8rem. body base is 14px (styles.css:86), so .status renders at 11.2px normal weight — below the large-text threshold (18.66px bold / 24px regular), so the 4.5:1 normal-text AA bar applies. ui.js setStatus() (lines 474-482) writes textContent and sets style.color='' (clearing any inline override), so live status messages ("Recording... Click again to stop" at ui.js:555, errors via showError 491-492) render in the muted token. My computed ratios: light #9CA3AF on #FFFFFF = 2.54:1; dark #6B7294 on #151929 = 3.71:1 — both fail 4.5:1.

spinner-text (styles.css:644-648) uses the same --text-muted on the surface card → identical 2.54:1 / 3.71:1, fail.

Placeholders (styles.css:718-720 transcript, 829-832 form input) use --text-muted on --bg-inset → 2.35:1 light / 3.37:1 dark, fail. (Placeholder text is a known SC 1.4.3 gray area, but the numbers are correct.)

save-button label (styles.css:853-867) is color:var(--bg-primary) on background:var(--accent): light #EEF0FB on #5B6EF5 = 3.70:1 (fails); dark #0C0F1A on #7B8FF7 = 6.47:1 (passes). The analyst correctly scoped this failure to light mode.

Minor inaccuracy that does not change the verdict: the analyst labels line 644 as panel-sublabel, but 644 is .spinner-text (.panel-sublabel is at line 225); both nonetheless use --text-muted, so the conclusion stands. Severity medium is right — a genuine, reproducible accessibility regression on the app's primary feedback channel, but not a functional break, and placeholder contrast is partially WCAG-exempt.

verifiedBugs.title: No prefers-reduced-motion handling for any animation

verdict.title: No prefers-reduced-motion handling for any animation
verdict.reasoning: I read the exact cited lines in css/styles.css and confirmed every animation. Spinner: line 636 `animation: spin 0.8s linear infinite` with `@keyframes spin` at 640-642 (continuous rotation). Card fade-in: lines 448-449 `.card-animate { animation: cardFadeIn 400ms ease-out both; animation-delay: calc(var(--card-index, 0) * 120ms); }` with keyframes at 452-461. Modal slide-up: line 757 `animation: modalSlideUp 300ms ease-out` with keyframes 760-769. Mic-icon: line 606 `transition: transform var(--transition)` plus hover (609-611) and active (613-615) translate/scale transforms. I verified these are live, not dead code: index.html:135 and :152 apply `card-animate` with `--card-index: 0` and `1`; index.html:192-194 renders the spinner, shown via ui.js:530 (`this.spinnerContainer.style.display = 'block'`); index.html:202 applies `modal-content` to the settings dialog. `grep -rn prefers-reduced-motion` across css/js/html returned exit 1 (zero matches), so no `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block exists anywhere to disable or shorten these. The stylesheet also has ~20 `transition:` declarations, all unconditional. The defect is real and confirmed: a user with the OS reduce-motion preference set will still see the infinite spinner, staggered card motion, modal slide, and mic micro-animations. I downgraded severity from medium to low because this is a non-functional accessibility/polish gap (WCAG 2.3.3 is AAA): no crash, no data loss, no broken functionality, and the motions are small/short. The original medium overstates impact for an app of this scope, but the underlying defect itself is unquestionably present.

verifiedBugs.title: Core control buttons have no visible keyboard focus indicator

verdict.title: Core control buttons have no app-supplied keyboard focus indicator (UA default outline still applies)
verdict.reasoning: I read css/styles.css and index.html directly and confirmed the factual core of the claim. A full grep of css/styles.css shows exactly four focus selectors: .panel-select:focus (257), .toggle-switch input:focus-visible (326), .transcript-content:focus (722), and .form-group input:focus/.form-group select:focus (822-823). None target the control buttons. Reading the cited button rules confirms they define only :hover (and for mic, :active/.recording/:disabled): .icon-btn (541-561, used by #pause-button/#retry-button/#cancel-button per index.html 161/179/185), .mic-button (571-621, #mic-button line 170), .header-btn/.panel-footer-btn (397-424, used by #panel-toggle line 109, #theme-toggle line 86, #settings-button line 98), and .clear-btn (672-700, the Cut button #grab-text-button line 139). So the claim that these controls have no app-defined :focus/:focus-visible rule is accurate, and the noise-toggle at 326 is indeed the only genuine :focus-visible style. The form/transcript/select controls do remove the outline (outline:none at 258/723/824) and substitute a faint --focus-ring (0 0 0 2px rgba(accent,0.15/0.20)), so there is a visible UI inconsistency. One important correction to severity: there is NO universal/global outline reset — every outline:none is scoped inside a specific :focus block that also adds its own ring. The buttons therefore retain the browser's native (UA) focus outline; keyboard focus on the record/pause/cancel/retry/theme/settings/Cut controls is NOT invisible, it just uses the browser default instead of the app's ring. That makes this a real consistency/polish gap rather than a WCAG 2.4.7 focus-visibility failure. The analyst's own description acknowledged this and chose 'likely' for exactly this reason, which the code confirms. I keep classification at likely but lower severity from medium to low, since focus remains perceivable via the UA outline and the impact is cosmetic inconsistency, not loss of keyboard focus visibility.

verifiedBugs.title: Tautological workflow tests verify their own mock instead of production checkRecordingPrerequisites
verifiedBugs.location: lines 236-243 and 284-291 ('Complete workflow integration with fixes')

verdict.title: Workflow tests mock checkRecordingPrerequisites, so its real mic-enable decision logic is untested
verdict.reasoning:
I read the two tests at tests/settings-workflow.vitest.js:236-243 and 284-291. Both do vi.spyOn(ui,'checkRecordingPrerequisites').mockImplementation(() => { const config = ...getModelConfig(); if (config.apiKey && config.uri) { ui.enableMicButton(); ui.setStatus(DEFAULT_RESET_STATUS); } return true; }). The subsequent assertions (lines 258-260 and 323-324) check enableMicButtonSpy was called and setStatus(DEFAULT_RESET_STATUS) was called. Those two assertions pass solely because the hand-written mock body calls them — they verify the mock, not production code.

I read the production method at js/ui.js:431-453. The real checkRecordingPrerequisites does: (1) browser-support short-circuit emitting APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED{ready:false,reason:'browser'} (lines 433-436); (2) config-missing path: setStatus(API_NOT_CONFIGURED) + disableMicButton + APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED{ready:false,reason:'config'} (lines 440-445); (3) success path: enableMicButton + setStatus(DEFAULT_RESET_STATUS) + APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED{ready:true} + APP_READY (lines 448-452). None of this runs in the two tests because the method is replaced. If the config check at line 440 were inverted, or enableMicButton/the APP_READY/APP_PREREQUISITES_CHECKED emissions were removed, both tests would still pass. So the tests cannot catch a regression in the method's body — the claim's core point is correct.

I verified the surrounding context to avoid over/under-stating: js/ui.js:279-286 wires SETTINGS_SAVED and SETTINGS_LOADED to call checkRecordingPrerequisites, and js/settings.js:692-712 (checkInitialSettings) and 642-643 (saveSettings) emit those events. So the assertion expect(checkRecordingPrerequisitesSpy).toHaveBeenCalled() is NOT tautological — it genuinely exercises real event-listener wiring. The tests are therefore partially tautological: wiring is covered, but the method's decision logic is not. The analyst's phrasing 'it cannot fail if checkRecordingPrerequisites regresses' is slightly too absolute, but the substantive defect (false coverage of the most important mic-enable decision) holds.

I grepped tests/ and confirmed checkRecordingPrerequisites is referenced only in settings-workflow.vitest.js. The only un-mocked spies on it (lines 330, 338) merely assert it was called on SETTINGS_UPDATED/SETTINGS_SAVED — they do not assert the resulting mic/status outcome either. I also checked tests/ui-event-bus-proper.vitest.js: its enableMicButton/disableMicButton assertions (lines 213/225/236/257) cover RECORDING_STATE_CHANGED handling, a different path, not the prerequisite decision logic. So the production decision logic in checkRecordingPrerequisites has no test coverage anywhere.

This is a real test-quality / false-coverage defect, confirmed by reading the faulty path. I lowered severity from high to medium: there is no production runtime impact (it is a test gap), and the event wiring portion does retain genuine coverage, so 'high' overstates it.

verifiedBugs.title: MediaRecorder 'error' cleanup test exercises a code path that does not exist
verifiedBugs.location: lines 415-449 ('cleans up resources after MediaRecorder errors')

verdict.title: MediaRecorder 'error' cleanup test exercises a code path that does not exist
verdict.reasoning:
I independently confirmed the claim by reading both files.

Mock setup (tests/audio-handler-integration.vitest.js:75-95): mediaRecorderEventHandlers is initialized with error: [] (line 80). The mock's addEventListener (lines 91-93) only pushes a handler into mediaRecorderEventHandlers[event] for the exact event name the production code registers.

Production registration (js/audio-handler.js:187-208): startRecording registers ONLY 'dataavailable' (line 187) and 'stop' (line 191) listeners. A repo-wide grep for onerror / addEventListener('error', ...) / mediaRecorder.on found no MediaRecorder error-event wiring anywhere in js/. The only RECORDING_ERROR emissions are in getUserMedia failure (line 169) and safeStopRecorder's try/catch (line 227) — neither is a MediaRecorder 'error' event handler.

Therefore at test line 424, mediaRecorderEventHandlers.error is the empty array initialized at line 80, and the forEach(handler => handler({ error })) iterates zero times — a guaranteed no-op. The simulated MediaRecorder error triggers no code path.

The cleanup the test asserts (lines 443-448: audioChunks length 0, timerInterval null, mediaRecorder null, state IDLE) is produced solely by the subsequent normal stopRecordingFlow() (line 432) and the dispatched 'stop' event (lines 435-437), which runs the stop handler at audio-handler.js:191-208 (transition to PROCESSING, processAndSendAudio, then cleanup at line 207). This is identical to the normal-stop flow exercised by other tests in the file. So the test passes but validates nothing about error handling; its name/intent ('cleans up resources after MediaRecorder errors') is vacuous and the real-browser MediaRecorder onerror scenario is untested.

Classification confirmed: the no-op forEach and absent error listener are both directly verified. I lowered severity from medium to low because this is a misleading/false-confidence test-quality issue, not a production runtime defect — the shipping code behaves correctly; only the test's coverage claim is false.

verifiedBugs.title: destroy() test hard-codes listener count (_unsubscribers.length === 5)

verdict.title: destroy() test hard-codes listener count (_unsubscribers.length === 5)
verdict.reasoning: I read tests/audio-handler-integration.vitest.js lines 544-558 and js/audio-handler.js lines 50-57 and 424-426. The factual premise of the claim is accurate: line 546 asserts expect(audioHandler._unsubscribers.length).toBe(5), and setupEventBusListeners() registers exactly 5 eventBus.on subscriptions (API_CONFIG_MISSING line 52, MIC_BUTTON_CLICKED 53, PAUSE_BUTTON_CLICKED 54, CANCEL_BUTTON_CLICKED 55, RETRY_BUTTON_CLICKED 56). The meaningful assertions the claim cites (lines 550-552: _unsubscribers emptied to [], API_CONFIG_MISSING listener removed from eventBus.events) are indeed present. However, this is NOT a defect. The test is currently correct and passes: there are exactly 5 listeners so toBe(5) matches the code. There is no incorrect production behavior, no failing assertion, and no runtime fault in destroy() (lines 424-426 iterate _unsubscribers and reset to []). The claim's own description admits the failure only materializes hypothetically if a future feature adds a sixth listener, explicitly stating this happens 'despite destroy() being correct' — i.e., production code is fine. A hard-coded count assertion that flags when the listener set changes is a defensible (if low-value) early-warning guard, not a bug; if it ever fails, a developer simply updates the number after confirming the destroy contract still holds. This is a test-style/maintainability preference, not a defect. No code path produces a wrong result. Marking not-a-bug, severity none.

verifiedBugs.title: mic-button-effects CSS-absence tests are order-dependent first-match string assertions

verdict.title: mic-button-effects CSS-absence tests are order-dependent first-match string assertions
verdict.reasoning:
I read tests/mic-button-effects.vitest.js (lines 1-40) and css/styles.css (the .mic-button blocks at lines 571, 589, 594, 609, 613, 618, 938, 987). The analyst's mechanical description is accurate: getCssBlock (lines 8-14) builds a non-global, non-anchored RegExp and returns match[0], the FIRST matching block; there are three literal `.mic-button` rule blocks so the first (line 571) wins. I reproduced this with Node and confirmed each selector resolves to the expected block. However, the analyst's TWO concrete failure scenarios — the basis for the "confirmed" label — are both wrong:

1. "Moving a media-query .mic-button block above line 571 flips the result with zero behavioral meaning." I simulated exactly this by prepending the media-query block (which contains only width/height, lines 938-941) to the top of the CSS. The `not.toMatch(/transition:\s*all\b/)` assertion (line 30) STILL PASSES, because that block has no transition. The assertion only flips when the first-matched block itself contains `transition: all` — and a `transition: all` on any .mic-button rule IS the exact regression being guarded against, so flipping then is behaviorally meaningful, not noise. The block-extraction's real weakness is the opposite: it could MISS a regression placed in a non-first block (false negative / too lenient), which is not what the claim describes.

2. "Re-introducing a non-expanding keyframe with a different name flips the result." Lines 22-23 and 38 are literal `css.not.toContain('glowPulse'|'ringExpand'|'@keyframes recordPulse')` checks against the WHOLE css string, not block extraction and not order-dependent. A differently-named keyframe would NOT match these strings, so the test would still PASS — nothing flips. This is again a missed-regression (lenient) concern, mischaracterized as a false failure.

Only lines 30 and 37 use block extraction; lines 22-23, 38 are whole-file string scans. `.mic-button.recording` (line 35) matches a literally unique selector (only line 618), so it is not order-sensitive at all. Running `npx vitest run` shows all 4 tests pass and correctly reflect the current CSS. There is no concrete failure path: no false positive (wrongful failure) is produced by any benign reorganization the analyst named, and no current defect exists. At most this is a mild test-quality observation (block extraction is a coarse helper that could silently under-detect), which does not rise to a real defect and certainly not a "confirmed" one — the offered failure scenarios do not occur. Classifying as not-a-bug.

verifiedBugs.title: Config source split-brain: getModelConfig reads a different selector than validation/visibility

verdict.title: Config source split-brain: getModelConfig reads a different selector than validation/visibility
verdict.reasoning:
I read js/settings.js in full around the cited lines and grepped the entire codebase (js/, index.html) for every read/write of the two selectors. The factual premises of the claim are correct but the asserted failure cannot occur in any current path.

Selector readers:
- getCurrentModel():659 returns this.modelSelect.value (main toolbar).
- getCurrentModelFromSettings():669-674 returns this.settingsModelSelect.value (modal), falling back to main.
- getModelConfig():682-690 reads model = getCurrentModel() (main) then pulls URI/key from localStorage keyed by THAT SAME model (687-688) — it never mixes one model's identity with another model's stored credentials.
- saveSettings():586 and getValidationErrors():550 and updateSettingsVisibility():414 use getCurrentModelFromSettings() (modal).

So validation/save use the modal selector, the recorder (audio-handler.js:116, api-client.js:266) uses the main selector. The whole claim reduces to: can settingsModelSelect.value diverge from modelSelect.value?

I enumerated EVERY writer of the two selectors (grep results, only 4 sites, no dispatchEvent anywhere, no external module touches them):
1. loadSavedModel():102-104 sets BOTH to savedModel — synced.
2. modelSelect 'change' listener:120-139 copies new value into settingsModelSelect at 130 (first statement, nothing can throw before it) — synced.
3. settingsModelSelect 'change' listener:142-158 copies new value into modelSelect at 152 — synced.
4. loadSettingsToForm():464-465 sets settingsModelSelect.value = getCurrentModel() (= modelSelect.value) — this is an explicit re-sync, not a desync.

The two direct-set sites the claim flags as fragile (loadSavedModel:102-105 and loadSettingsToForm:464-465) both PRESERVE the invariant: the first writes both selectors, the second forces the modal to match the main selector. Neither can produce divergence. The claim itself concedes "Not proven to fire in the current flows." There is no code path — user-driven or programmatic — where the selectors hold different values at the moment getModelConfig is consulted, so the "MAI credentials saved while recorder pulls Whisper" scenario is unreachable.

This is a code-style observation (two selectors instead of one source of truth) rather than a defect. No incorrect behavior is reachable today; isReal=false, classification not-a-bug. I left severity at low only to reflect the legitimate maintainability concern of the dual-selector mirroring pattern, but there is no functional bug.

verifiedBugs.title: Single failure drives the status element twice via two independent error channels
verifiedBugs.location: startRecordingFlow catch block (141-148) + recording-state-machine.handleErrorState (271-280) + error-handler ERROR_OCCURRED listener (ui.js:259)

verdict.title: Single failure drives the status element twice via two independent error channels
verdict.reasoning:
I traced the full path across four files and confirm the double-write to the same status element.

1. audio-handler.js:142 — in the startRecordingFlow catch, errorHandler.handleError(err, {module:'AudioHandler'}) runs. ErrorHandler.handleError (error-handler.js:17-22) synchronously emits APP_EVENTS.ERROR_OCCURRED with {code, message, context}.

2. eventBus.emit (event-bus.js:100-134) is fully synchronous: it iterates listeners with forEach and calls listener.callback(data) inline. So the ERROR_OCCURRED listener fires before handleError returns.

3. The ERROR_OCCURRED listener (ui.js:259-262) calls this.showError(message). showError (ui.js:491-493) calls setStatus(message), which sets this.statusElement.textContent = message (ui.js:480) — the raw err.message.

4. Back in audio-handler.js:145, await this.stateMachine.transitionTo(RECORDING_STATES.ERROR, {error: errorMessage}) runs. transitionTo (recording-state-machine.js:86-111) invokes the registered ERROR handler at line 105-108 (stateHandlers[RECORDING_STATES.ERROR] = handleErrorState.bind(this), registered at line 50).

5. handleErrorState (recording-state-machine.js:271-280) emits UI_STATUS_UPDATE with message = `${MESSAGES.ERROR_PREFIX}${errorMessage}. ${MESSAGES.TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY}` = "Error: <msg>. Tap mic to retry" (constants.js: ERROR_PREFIX='Error: ', TAP_MIC_TO_RETRY='Tap mic to retry').

6. The UI_STATUS_UPDATE listener (ui.js:185-197) takes the non-temporary branch (no data.temporary) and calls this.setStatus(data.message) at line 195, writing the formatted string to the SAME this.statusElement.textContent, overwriting the raw message from step 3.

Conclusion: for one underlying error, the status element is written twice (raw message, then formatted "Error: ... Tap mic to retry"), last-writer-wins. The first write is wasted work and the two error subsystems redundantly target the same UI element, exactly as claimed.

Severity correctly remains low: the user ends up seeing the correct, better-formatted final message, so there is no functional/UX defect — only redundant work and an architectural ownership smell. One fairness caveat: the ERROR_OCCURRED channel is not entirely useless globally (it also drives logger.error at error-handler.js:20 and any other ERROR_OCCURRED consumers), but the claim is specifically about the duplicate status-element write, which is concretely real.

verifiedBugs.title: README misdescribes which git hook runs lint

verdict.title: README misdescribes which git hook runs lint (pre-push vs pre-commit)
verdict.reasoning: I read all the cited files directly. README.md:64-71 (Git Hooks section) states under "Pre-push hooks automatically run:" three items, including line 67 "- Linting checks (`npm run lint`)", plus line 68 coverage and line 69 deps validation. However, /home/ahmedmuhi/code/whisper-transcribe/.husky/pre-push contains exactly one line: `npm run test:coverage && npm run deps:check:prod` — no lint invocation. Lint is run by /home/ahmedmuhi/code/whisper-transcribe/.husky/pre-commit, which contains exactly `npm run lint`. I confirmed via package.json that all three scripts are real and distinct: line 17 "lint": "eslint \"js/**/*.js\"", line 9 "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage", line 20 "deps:check:prod": "knip --production --dependencies". So the README is not merely calling the same script under a different label — lint genuinely runs at commit time, not push time. The mismatch is real: a contributor trusting the README would believe lint is gated at push; if they bypassed the commit hook (e.g. git commit --no-verify), lint-failing code could be pushed because the pre-push hook never runs eslint. This is a documentation/behavior mismatch with no runtime crash, so severity low is appropriate (kept as claimed). The claim's only minor imprecision is that the README header reads "Pre-push hooks automatically run" rather than literally "pre-push runs lint," but it explicitly enumerates lint under the pre-push heading, so the substance of the defect holds.

verifiedBugs.title: README undercounts/mislabels supported transcription models
verifiedBugs.location: README.md:9 (Features) and 31-37 (Configuration) vs js/model-adapters/ and spec REQ-002

verdict.title: README undercounts/mislabels supported transcription models
verdict.reasoning:
I read README.md:9 ("Multiple Models: Azure Whisper (stable) and MAI-Transcribe (preview)") and README.md:33 ("Select your transcription model (Azure Whisper or MAI-Transcribe)"). I confirmed js/model-adapters/whisper-translate.js exists and is registered in js/model-adapters/index.js:13, and that spec/spec-design-api-client.md REQ-002 (line 42) lists three models. So the claim's factual premise (a third adapter exists in code/spec) is true.

However, the claim's asserted harm — "a user selecting it has no guidance on its behavior" — is not reachable. The model picker is static HTML: index.html lines 77-80 and 209-213 define the dropdowns with exactly two <option> values: "whisper" (Whisper (Stable)) and "mai-transcribe" (MAI-Transcribe (Preview)). Neither dropdown contains a whisper-translate option. I verified the dropdowns are NOT dynamically populated from modelAdapterRegistry: settings.js only reads the saved value and sets this.modelSelect.value = savedModel (loadSavedModel, lines 100-105) and syncs the two selects on change (lines 120-159); there is no code that appends options from the registry. So a normal user CANNOT select whisper-translate through the UI. The only way to reach it would be manually editing localStorage's MODEL key, which is not a documented or normal user action.

REQ-002 is explicitly scoped to the AzureAPIClient component's internal capability (spec sections 1-2 frame it as an API-client design requirement for developers/QA), not to which models the user-facing picker exposes. whisper-translate is therefore an internal/developer-facing adapter, not a user-selectable feature.

Because the README is a user-facing document and the two models it lists are exactly the two the UI lets a user select, the README accurately reflects the user-facing reality. The claimed failure mode (user picks whisper-translate, gets no guidance) cannot occur. At most this is a developer-doc completeness nitpick with no user impact, so the specific defect as described is not real.

verifiedBugs.title: package.json declares no license while repo has no LICENSE file
verifiedBugs.location: package.json:1-51 (no license field) + repo root (no LICENSE)

verdict.title: package.json has no "license" field and repo has no LICENSE file
verdict.reasoning:
I read all three relevant artifacts firsthand. package.json (lines 1-51) contains no "license" field; line 4 is "private": true and line 3 is "version": "1.0.0". A repo-wide ls/find/git ls-files for licen*/COPYING returned nothing tracked (the grep hits were only the literal word "license" appearing in plan/spec prose under plan/archive and spec/, not license grants). README.md (full read, 94 lines) has no license/copyright section, and lines 84-90 actively invite "Fork or clone this repository" plus GitHub Pages hosting, which under default copyright ("all rights reserved") those parties have no granted right to do. So the analyst's factual observations are all accurate and confirmed.

However, this is not a code defect. It is a licensing/release-governance metadata omission with zero impact on runtime behavior, correctness, security, or any executable code path. There is no faulty code path to traverse. The claim also overstates context: it frames this as a "2.0 public release" blocker, but the package is version 1.0.0 and explicitly "private": true, so the "2.0 public release" premise is an unread assumption about future intent. For a code-review/defect context the correct label is not-a-bug (it is real as a documentation gap but is not a software defect), and severity is low rather than medium because nothing functional, secure, or behavioral is affected — it is purely a project-hygiene/legal-clarity item.
